topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET501 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Deciles, Quartiles and Percentiles, Descriptive Statistics: Measures of Central Tendency, Measures of Variability, Skewness and Kurtosis, Chebyshev's Theorem, the Empirical Rule, Exploratory Data Analysis, Overview of Discrete and Continuous Probability Distributions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Deciles, Quartiles and Percentiles (Position Statistics)

### Formal Definition
**Quartiles**, **Deciles**, and **Percentiles** are measures of position (also called location statistics or order statistics) that divide an ordered dataset into equal parts. They indicate the value below which a given percentage of observations fall.

- **Quartiles** divide data into **4 equal parts** (Q1, Q2, Q3). Q1 = 25th percentile, Q2 = 50th percentile (median), Q3 = 75th percentile.
- **Deciles** divide data into **10 equal parts** (D1, D2, ..., D9). Dk = k·10th percentile.
- **Percentiles** divide data into **100 equal parts** (P1, P2, ..., P99). Pk = k-th percentile.

The general position formula for the k-th percentile in a sorted dataset of size n is:

$$P_k = \text{value at position } \frac{k(n+1)}{100}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** KTU Board Examiners frequently test the distinction between *exclusive* and *inclusive* percentile calculation methods. Always state which method you are using in the answer script.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Class Rank" Metaphor
Imagine 100 students standing in ascending order of height. The 25th percentile (Q1) is the student who has exactly 25 students shorter than them — they are at the "25% height marker." Similarly, the 90th percentile (D9 or P90) means you are taller than 90% of the class. In data analytics, these metrics tell you *"where does a value stand relative to the rest of the data?"*

---

## 1.2 Descriptive Statistics — Measures of Central Tendency

### Formal Definition
**Measures of Central Tendency** are single-value summaries that identify the central or "typical" position of a dataset. They answer the question: *"What is a representative value of the data?"*

The three principal measures are:
- **Mean (Arithmetic Average)** — Sum of all observations divided by the number of observations.
- **Median** — The middle value when data is arranged in ascending/descending order.
- **Mode** — The most frequently occurring value in the dataset.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mean** is sensitive to outliers; **Median** is robust (resistant) to outliers; **Mode** is the only measure useful for categorical/nominal data.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Center of Mass"
Think of a seesaw with values placed along its length. The **mean** is the exact balance point if all values had equal weight. The **median** is the middle person in a queue. The **mode** is the most popular dish ordered at a canteen on Monday. Each tells a different "center story."

---

## 1.3 Measures of Variability (Dispersion)

### Formal Definition
**Measures of Variability** (also called measures of dispersion or spread) quantify the degree to which data points are spread out from the central value. They answer: *"How scattered or concentrated is the data?"*

Key measures include:
- **Range** = Max − Min
- **Variance (σ² or s²)** — Average of squared deviations from the mean.
- **Standard Deviation (σ or s)** — Square root of variance; expressed in original units.
- **Interquartile Range (IQR)** = Q3 − Q1 — Robust measure of spread.
- **Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD)** — Average of absolute deviations from the mean.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Board Standard: Population parameters use Greek letters (**μ**, **σ²**, **σ**); Sample statistics use Latin letters (**x̄**, **s²**, **s**). Mixing them costs marks.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Height of Buildings in Two Cities"
City A has buildings of heights 50, 51, 52, 51, 50 m (low spread). City B has buildings of heights 10, 100, 30, 80, 90 m (high spread). Both have the same mean (~51m), but their **standard deviations** are vastly different. Variability measures capture this hidden story.

---

## 1.4 Skewness and Kurtosis (Shape Statistics)

### Formal Definition
**Skewness** is a measure of the asymmetry of a probability distribution around its mean. **Kurtosis** is a measure of the "tailedness" (heaviness of tails and peak sharpness) of a distribution.

- **Skewness = 0** → Symmetric (e.g., Normal Distribution)
- **Skewness > 0** → Positively skewed (right tail longer)
- **Skewness < 0** → Negatively skewed (left tail longer)
- **Kurtosis = 3 (mesokurtic)** → Normal-like tails
- **Kurtosis > 3 (leptokurtic)** → Heavy tails, sharp peak
- **Kurtosis < 3 (platykurtic)** → Light tails, flat peak

> [!NOTE]
> Many software packages report **excess kurtosis** = Kurtosis − 3, so a normal distribution has excess kurtosis = 0.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Salary Distribution"
Income distributions are typically **positively skewed** — most people earn modest salaries (clustered left), with a few billionaires stretching the right tail. **Kurtosis** is like the "weight" of extreme events — high kurtosis means rare catastrophic events (financial crashes) happen more often than a normal bell curve predicts.

---

## 1.5 Chebyshev's Theorem

### Formal Definition
**Chebyshev's Theorem** provides a lower bound on the proportion of data that lies within k standard deviations of the mean for **any distribution** (regardless of shape). For any k > 1:

$$\text{At least } \left(1 - \frac{1}{k^2}\right) \times 100\% \text{ of data lies within } \pm k \text{ standard deviations of the mean}$$

Valid only for k > 1. For k = 1, the bound is 0 (trivial).

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Universal Safety Net"
Unlike the Empirical Rule (which works only for bell-shaped data), Chebyshev's Theorem is a **distribution-agnostic safety net**. It says, "No matter how weird your data is, at least 75% of it must lie within 2 standard deviations." It's a worst-case guarantee.

---

## 1.6 The Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7 Rule)

### Formal Definition
The **Empirical Rule** applies **only to bell-shaped (approximately normal) distributions** and states:
- **~68%** of data lies within **±1σ** of the mean
- **~95%** of data lies within **±2σ** of the mean
- **~99.7%** of data lies within **±3σ** of the mean

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Bell Curve Zones"
Imagine a bell-shaped mountain. The middle 68% of hikers are within 1 km of the summit. The middle 95% are within 2 km. The middle 99.7% are within 3 km. The remaining 0.3% are the "outliers" — extreme adventurers far from the peak.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Pitfall: Students often apply the Empirical Rule to skewed data and lose marks. The Empirical Rule requires **symmetric, bell-shaped** data. For other distributions, **use Chebyshev's Theorem**.

---

## 1.7 Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)

### Formal Definition
**Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)** is the critical first step in any data analytics pipeline. Coined by **John Tukey** (1977), EDA uses summary statistics, visualizations (box plots, histograms, scatter plots), and pattern-detection techniques to:
1. Understand the structure of data
2. Detect outliers and anomalies
3. Test preliminary hypotheses
4. Inform model selection

> [!NOTE]
> EDA is **non-confirmatory** — it explores, it does not prove. It precedes formal inferential statistics.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Detective at a Crime Scene"
A detective arriving at a crime scene (the dataset) doesn't immediately arrest a suspect. They walk around, take photographs, dust for fingerprints, and identify clues. EDA is this initial investigation — finding patterns, anomalies, and leads before formal hypothesis testing.

---

## 1.8 Overview of Discrete and Continuous Probability Distributions

### Formal Definition
A **Probability Distribution** is a mathematical function that gives the probabilities of occurrence of different possible outcomes for an experiment.

- **Discrete Probability Distribution** — Outcomes are countable (integers or distinct categories). Defined by a **Probability Mass Function (PMF)** P(X = x). Sum of all probabilities = 1. Examples: Binomial, Poisson, Geometric, Bernoulli.
- **Continuous Probability Distribution** — Outcomes form a continuum (real numbers in an interval). Defined by a **Probability Density Function (PDF)** f(x). Area under the curve = 1. Examples: Normal, Exponential, Uniform, Gamma.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For continuous distributions, P(X = x) = 0 for any specific x. Probabilities are computed over **intervals** using integration.

### Conceptual Analogy — "Counting Apples vs. Measuring Water"
- **Discrete** = "How many apples did you pick?" — You count 0, 1, 2, 3... whole numbers.
- **Continuous** = "How much water is in the bottle?" — 1.234... liters — infinitely divisible, so probability is computed over ranges, not single points.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Steps — Computing Position Statistics

### Step 1: Sort the Data
Arrange observations in ascending order: $x_1 \le x_2 \le x_3 \le ... \le x_n$

### Step 2: Compute the Locator (Position Index)
For the k-th percentile:
$$L = \frac{k}{100} \times (n+1)$$
Or using the alternative method (more common in KTU):
$$L = \frac{k}{100} \times n$$
then round up to the next integer for the position.

### Step 3: Locate the Value
If L is an integer, the percentile is the value at position L. If L is a fraction, interpolate between the two nearest values.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "Why":** Position statistics serve three critical engineering purposes — outlier detection (box plots use IQR), benchmarking (where does a value stand?), and quality control (Six Sigma uses percentile-based control limits).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Table 1: Measures of Central Tendency

| Statistic | Population Formula | Sample Formula | Data Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Arithmetic Mean** | $\mu = \dfrac{\sum x_i}{N}$ | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum x_i}{n}$ | Interval/Ratio |
| **Median** | Middle value of sorted data | Middle value of sorted data | Ordinal and above |
| **Mode** | Most frequent value | Most frequent value | Nominal and above |
| **Geometric Mean** | $\left(\prod x_i\right)^{1/N}$ | $\left(\prod x_i\right)^{1/n}$ | Growth rates, ratios |
| **Harmonic Mean** | $\dfrac{N}{\sum (1/x_i)}$ | $\dfrac{n}{\sum (1/x_i)}$ | Rates, speeds |

### Table 2: Measures of Variability

| Statistic | Population Formula | Sample Formula |
|---|---|---|
| **Range** | $R = x_{max} - x_{min}$ | $R = x_{max} - x_{min}$ |
| **Variance** | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \mu)^2}{N}$ | $s^2 = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}$ |
| **Standard Deviation** | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$ | $s = \sqrt{s^2}$ |
| **IQR** | $IQR = Q_3 - Q_1$ | $IQR = Q_3 - Q_1$ |
| **MAD** | $\dfrac{1}{N}\sum \vert x_i - \mu \vert$ | $\dfrac{1}{n}\sum \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert$ |
| **CV (Coefficient of Variation)** | $\dfrac{\sigma}{\mu} \times 100\%$ | $\dfrac{s}{\bar{x}} \times 100\%$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Use $n-1$ (Bessel's correction) for **sample** variance to obtain an **unbiased estimator**. Using $n$ in the denominator is a frequent error costing 1-2 marks.

### Table 3: Shape Statistics

| Statistic | Formula (Sample) | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Skewness (Pearson's)** | $Sk = \dfrac{3(\bar{x} - \text{Median})}{s}$ | Symmetric if = 0; right if > 0; left if < 0 |
| **Skewness (Moment-based)** | $g_1 = \dfrac{\frac{1}{n}\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^3}{s^3}$ | Same interpretation |
| **Kurtosis (Moment-based)** | $g_2 = \dfrac{\frac{1}{n}\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^4}{s^4}$ | Normal = 3; heavy tails if > 3 |
| **Excess Kurtosis** | $g_2 - 3$ | Normal = 0 |

### Table 4: Chebyshev's Theorem & Empirical Rule

| Rule | Applicability | Coverage |
|---|---|---|
| **Empirical Rule** | Bell-shaped (normal) | 68% within 1σ; 95% within 2σ; 99.7% within 3σ |
| **Chebyshev's Theorem** | Any distribution | At least $\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{k^2}\right)\%$ within kσ for k > 1 |

### Table 5: Discrete and Continuous Distributions

| Property | Discrete Distribution | Continuous Distribution |
|---|---|---|
| **Function** | PMF: $P(X = x_i)$ | PDF: $f(x)$ |
| **Total Probability** | $\sum P(X = x_i) = 1$ | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx = 1$ |
| **Cumulative** | $F(x) = P(X \le x)$ | $F(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t)\,dt$ |
| **Expected Value** | $E(X) = \sum x_i P(x_i)$ | $E(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x f(x)\,dx$ |
| **Variance** | $Var(X) = \sum (x_i - \mu)^2 P(x_i)$ | $Var(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (x - \mu)^2 f(x)\,dx$ |
| **Examples** | Binomial, Poisson, Geometric | Normal, Exponential, Uniform |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **EDA in Industry:** Used in semiconductor manufacturing to detect process drift before defects occur.
- **Chebyshev vs Empirical Rule:** Quality engineers use Chebyshev for any production line; the Empirical Rule is reserved for mature, stable processes.
- **Skewness/Kurtosis in Finance:** Risk analysts use these to assess whether stock returns follow normal distributions (most don't — they have "fat tails").
- **Discrete vs Continuous:** Network packet counts → Discrete (Poisson); Signal amplitudes → Continuous (Normal/Gaussian).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Quartiles, Deciles, Percentiles

**Problem:** Find Q1, Q2, Q3, D7, and P85 for the dataset: 12, 15, 18, 22, 25, 28, 31, 35, 40, 45 (n = 10)

### Step 1: Sort the Data
Data is already sorted in ascending order. n = 10.

### Step 2: Compute Q1 (25th Percentile)
Using $L = \dfrac{k(n+1)}{100}$:

$$L_{Q_1} = \frac{25 \times 11}{100} = 2.75$$

Since L = 2.75 is not an integer, we interpolate between the 2nd and 3rd values:

$$Q_1 = x_2 + 0.75 \times (x_3 - x_2) = 15 + 0.75 \times (18 - 15)$$

$$Q_1 = 15 + 0.75 \times 3 = 15 + 2.25 = 17.25$$

### Step 3: Compute Q2 (50th Percentile — Median)
$$L_{Q_2} = \frac{50 \times 11}{100} = 5.5$$

$$Q_2 = x_5 + 0.5 \times (x_6 - x_5) = 25 + 0.5 \times (28 - 25) = 25 + 1.5 = 26.5$$

### Step 4: Compute Q3 (75th Percentile)
$$L_{Q_3} = \frac{75 \times 11}{100} = 8.25$$

$$Q_3 = x_8 + 0.25 \times (x_9 - x_8) = 35 + 0.25 \times (40 - 35) = 35 + 1.25 = 36.25$$

### Step 5: Compute D7 (70th Percentile)
$$L_{D_7} = \frac{70 \times 11}{100} = 7.7$$

$$D_7 = x_7 + 0.7 \times (x_8 - x_7) = 31 + 0.7 \times (35 - 31) = 31 + 2.8 = 33.8$$

### Step 6: Compute P85 (85th Percentile)
$$L_{P_{85}} = \frac{85 \times 11}{100} = 9.35$$

$$P_{85} = x_9 + 0.35 \times (x_{10} - x_9) = 40 + 0.35 \times (45 - 40) = 40 + 1.75 = 41.75$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** When L is fractional, we linearly interpolate between $\lfloor L \rfloor$ and $\lceil L \rceil$ positions. The fraction $L - \lfloor L \rfloor$ is the interpolation weight.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Mean, Variance, Standard Deviation

**Problem:** For the dataset {4, 8, 6, 5, 3, 8, 9, 2}, compute mean, variance, and standard deviation (as a sample).

### Step 1: Compute the Mean
$$\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x_i}{n} = \frac{4 + 8 + 6 + 5 + 3 + 8 + 9 + 2}{8} = \frac{45}{8} = 5.625$$

### Step 2: Compute Deviations from Mean
Build a deviations table:

| $x_i$ | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ |
|---|---|---|
| 4 | -1.625 | 2.6406 |
| 8 | 2.375 | 5.6406 |
| 6 | 0.375 | 0.1406 |
| 5 | -0.625 | 0.3906 |
| 3 | -2.625 | 6.8906 |
| 8 | 2.375 | 5.6406 |
| 9 | 3.375 | 11.3906 |
| 2 | -3.625 | 13.1406 |
| **Sum** | **0.000** | **45.875** |

### Step 3: Compute Sample Variance
$$s^2 = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1} = \frac{45.875}{8-1} = \frac{45.875}{7} = 6.5536$$

### Step 4: Compute Sample Standard Deviation
$$s = \sqrt{s^2} = \sqrt{6.5536} = 2.5600$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of Step 1 Logic:** The sum of deviations $\sum(x_i - \bar{x})$ MUST equal 0 (or be negligibly close due to rounding). This is a key KTU verification check.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Skewness and Kurtosis

**Problem:** Compute Pearson's skewness and moment-based kurtosis for {2, 4, 6, 8, 10}.

### Step 1: Compute Mean and Median
$$\bar{x} = \frac{2+4+6+8+10}{5} = 6.0$$
$$\text{Median} = 6.0$$

### Step 2: Compute Standard Deviation (Sample)
Deviations: -4, -2, 0, 2, 4
Squared deviations: 16, 4, 0, 4, 16. Sum = 40.

$$s^2 = \frac{40}{5-1} = 10, \quad s = \sqrt{10} = 3.1623$$

### Step 3: Compute Pearson's Skewness
$$Sk = \frac{3(\bar{x} - \text{Median})}{s} = \frac{3(6 - 6)}{3.1623} = 0$$

The data is perfectly symmetric, so skewness = 0 (as expected).

### Step 4: Compute Moment-Based Kurtosis
$$g_2 = \frac{\frac{1}{n}\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^4}{s^4}$$

Compute fourth power deviations: 256, 16, 0, 16, 256. Sum = 544.

$$\frac{1}{n}\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^4 = \frac{544}{5} = 108.8$$

$$s^4 = (3.1623)^4 = 100$$

$$g_2 = \frac{108.8}{100} = 1.088$$

> [!NOTE]
> Wait — this seems low. **Important:** A uniform-like dataset has low kurtosis. The uniform distribution has excess kurtosis = -1.2, so total kurtosis ≈ 1.8. Our value of 1.088 is consistent with a flat, light-tailed distribution (platykurtic).

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Chebyshev's Theorem

**Problem:** A dataset has mean = 50 and standard deviation = 4. Using Chebyshev's Theorem, what is the minimum percentage of data between 42 and 58?

### Step 1: Identify the Range in Standard Deviation Units
The interval 42 to 58 is 50 ± 8, which is 50 ± 2σ. So k = 2.

### Step 2: Apply Chebyshev's Theorem
$$\text{Minimum \%} = \left(1 - \frac{1}{k^2}\right) \times 100\% = \left(1 - \frac{1}{4}\right) \times 100\% = 75\%$$

At least 75% of data lies between 42 and 58.

### Step 3: Apply the Empirical Rule (if data is normal)
If the distribution is bell-shaped, ~95% of data lies within ±2σ, so between 42 and 58 we expect ~95%.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic:** Chebyshev gives a **minimum** (worst-case) bound; the Empirical Rule gives an **approximate** value for normal data. The actual percentage is between 75% and 95% — we cannot be more specific without knowing the distribution's shape.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — EDA on Sample Data

**Problem:** Given the dataset {2, 3, 5, 5, 7, 8, 10, 12, 15, 100}, perform an EDA.

### Step 1: Compute Descriptive Statistics
- **Mean** = (2+3+5+5+7+8+10+12+15+100)/10 = 167/10 = 16.7
- **Median** = (7+8)/2 = 7.5
- **Mode** = 5 (appears twice)
- **Range** = 100 - 2 = 98
- **Variance** = Σ(xi - 16.7)² / 9 = (216.09+187.69+136.89+136.89+94.09+75.69+44.89+22.09+2.89+6939.29)/9 = 7856.50/9 = 872.94
- **Std Dev** = √872.94 = 29.55

### Step 2: Compute Quartiles and IQR
- **Q1** = position (25×11)/100 = 2.75 → x2 + 0.75(x3-x2) = 3 + 0.75(2) = 4.5
- **Q3** = position (75×11)/100 = 8.25 → x8 + 0.25(x9-x8) = 12 + 0.25(3) = 12.75
- **IQR** = 12.75 - 4.5 = 8.25

### Step 3: Detect Outliers (1.5 × IQR Rule)
- Lower Fence = Q1 - 1.5×IQR = 4.5 - 12.375 = -7.875
- Upper Fence = Q3 + 1.5×IQR = 12.75 + 12.375 = 25.125

**Conclusion:** 100 is an outlier (above upper fence). The dataset is **highly right-skewed** (mean 16.7 >> median 7.5).

### Step 4: EDA Visualization (Box Plot Interpretation)
- Box: spans Q1 (4.5) to Q3 (12.75), with median line at 7.5
- Whiskers extend to the smallest/largest non-outlier values (2 and 15)
- Outlier marker at 100

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The 1.5×IQR rule is a **Tukey's fence** — it provides an objective, distribution-free outlier detection method based purely on quartiles.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Measure

| Scenario | Recommended Central Tendency | Recommended Dispersion |
|---|---|---|
| Symmetric, no outliers | **Mean** | **Standard Deviation** |
| Skewed data or outliers present | **Median** | **IQR** |
| Categorical/Nominal data | **Mode** | Not meaningful |
| Comparing two datasets with different units | **CV (Relative)** | **CV** |
| Quality control (any distribution) | Median | Chebyshev bounds |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Map of Descriptive Statistics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Descriptive Statistics] --> B[Position Statistics]
    A --> C[Central Tendency]
    A --> D[Variability]
    A --> E[Shape Statistics]

    B --> B1[Quartiles Q1 Q2 Q3]
    B --> B2[Deciles D1 to D9]
    B --> B3[Percentiles P1 to P99]

    C --> C1[Mean]
    C --> C2[Median]
    C --> C3[Mode]

    D --> D1[Range]
    D --> D2[Variance]
    D --> D3[Standard Deviation]
    D --> D4[IQR]
    D --> D5[MAD]
    D --> D6[CV]

    E --> E1[Skewness]
    E --> E2[Kurtosis]

    E1 --> F1[Symmetric Equal]
    E1 --> F2[Right Skewed Positive]
    E1 --> F3[Left Skewed Negative]

    E2 --> G1[Mesokurtic Normal Like]
    E2 --> G2[Leptokurtic Heavy Tails]
    E2 --> G3[Platykurtic Light Tails]

    D6 --> H[Cross Dataset Comparison]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Flow — EDA Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Dataset] --> B[Data Cleaning]
    B --> C[Univariate Analysis]
    B --> D[Multivariate Analysis]

    C --> C1[Summary Stats]
    C --> C2[Histogram]
    C --> C3[Box Plot]

    D --> D1[Scatter Plot]
    D --> D2[Correlation Matrix]
    D --> D3[Crosstabs]

    C1 --> E[Outlier Detection]
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E

    E --> F{Outliers Found?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Investigate and Treat]
    F -->|No| H[Proceed to Modeling]
    G --> H

    D2 --> I[Pattern Discovery]
    D3 --> I
    I --> H

    H --> J[Inferential Statistics]
    J --> K[Final Model Deployment]
```

## 4.3 Probability Distribution Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Probability Distributions] --> B[Discrete]
    A --> C[Continuous]

    B --> B1[Bernoulli]
    B --> B2[Binomial]
    B --> B3[Poisson]
    B --> B4[Geometric]

    C --> C1[Uniform]
    C --> C2[Normal Gaussian]
    C --> C3[Exponential]
    C --> C4[Gamma]
    C --> C5[Beta]

    B2 --> D1[PMF P X equals x]
    B3 --> D1
    B4 --> D1
    B1 --> D1

    C1 --> D2[PDF f of x]
    C2 --> D2
    C3 --> D2
    C4 --> D2
    C5 --> D2

    D1 --> E1[Sum equals 1]
    D2 --> E2[Area under curve equals 1]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Chebyshev vs Empirical Rule

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Given Dataset] --> B{Is distribution bell shaped normal?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Use Empirical Rule]
    B -->|No| D[Use Chebyshev Theorem]
    B -->|Unknown| E[Use Chebyshev Theorem Safe]

    C --> F[68 percent within 1 sigma]
    C --> G[95 percent within 2 sigma]
    C --> H[99.7 percent within 3 sigma]

    D --> I[At least 1 minus 1 over k squared percent within k sigma]
    E --> I

    I --> J[Valid for k greater than 1]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Q: Define the Empirical Rule. State its applicability and the percentages associated with ±1σ, ±2σ, and ±3σ.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Empirical Rule** (also called the 68-95-99.7 Rule) applies to data that is **approximately bell-shaped** (i.e., follows a normal distribution). It states:

- Approximately **68%** of the data lies within **±1 standard deviation** of the mean.
- Approximately **95%** of the data lies within **±2 standard deviations** of the mean.
- Approximately **99.7%** of the data lies within **±3 standard deviations** of the mean.

**Applicability:** Only for symmetric, unimodal, bell-shaped distributions. For non-normal data, Chebyshev's Theorem should be used instead.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Q: Differentiate between PMF and PDF with two examples for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | PMF (Probability Mass Function) | PDF (Probability Density Function) |
|---|---|---|
| **Used for** | Discrete random variables | Continuous random variables |
| **Definition** | $P(X = x_i)$ — gives probability directly | $f(x)$ — probability density at point x |
| **Total Probability** | $\sum P(x_i) = 1$ | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx = 1$ |
| **Point Probability** | $P(X = x_i) > 0$ possible | $P(X = x) = 0$ for any single point |
| **Examples** | Binomial, Poisson | Normal, Exponential |

**Examples:**
- **PMF:** Binomial — number of heads in 10 coin tosses; Poisson — number of calls per hour at a call center.
- **PDF:** Normal — heights of students; Exponential — waiting time between bus arrivals.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** The marks obtained by 10 students in a class test are: 23, 45, 67, 12, 89, 34, 56, 78, 90, 41. Compute the mean, median, mode, range, variance, and standard deviation (as a sample).

**(b) [7 Marks]** From the above data, compute Pearson's coefficient of skewness. Comment on the shape of the distribution.

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — Descriptive Statistics

**Step 1: Sort the data in ascending order**
Sorted: 12, 23, 34, 41, 45, 56, 67, 78, 89, 90
n = 10, Σxᵢ = 535

**Step 2: Compute the Mean**
$$\bar{x} = \frac{535}{10} = 53.5$$

**Step 3: Compute the Median**
For n = 10 (even), median = average of 5th and 6th values:
$$\text{Median} = \frac{x_5 + x_6}{2} = \frac{45 + 56}{2} = 50.5$$

**Step 4: Compute the Mode**
No value repeats → **No mode** (or "uniform mode" — all values are unique). For a KTU answer, state: *The dataset is multimodal-free; no mode exists as all values are distinct.*

**Step 5: Compute the Range**
$$R = 90 - 12 = 78$$

**Step 6: Compute Deviations and Squared Deviations**

| $x_i$ | $x_i - 53.5$ | $(x_i - 53.5)^2$ |
|---|---|---|
| 12 | -41.5 | 1722.25 |
| 23 | -30.5 | 930.25 |
| 34 | -19.5 | 380.25 |
| 41 | -12.5 | 156.25 |
| 45 | -8.5 | 72.25 |
| 56 | 2.5 | 6.25 |
| 67 | 13.5 | 182.25 |
| 78 | 24.5 | 600.25 |
| 89 | 35.5 | 1260.25 |
| 90 | 36.5 | 1332.25 |
| **Sum** | **0.0** | **6642.50** |

**Step 7: Compute Sample Variance and Standard Deviation**
$$s^2 = \frac{6642.50}{10-1} = \frac{6642.50}{9} = 738.06$$

$$s = \sqrt{738.06} = 27.17$$

---

#### Part (b) — Pearson's Skewness

**Step 1: Recall Pearson's Coefficient of Skewness**
$$Sk_P = \frac{3(\bar{x} - \text{Median})}{s}$$

**Step 2: Substitute Values**
$$Sk_P = \frac{3(53.5 - 50.5)}{27.17} = \frac{3 \times 3.0}{27.17} = \frac{9.0}{27.17} = 0.3312$$

**Step 3: Interpretation**
Since $Sk_P = +0.3312 > 0$, the distribution is **moderately positively skewed** (right-skewed). This means:
- The right tail is longer than the left tail.
- The mean (53.5) is greater than the median (50.5), confirming positive skew.
- A few higher values (78, 89, 90) are pulling the mean to the right.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb:** $|Sk| < 0.5$ → approximately symmetric; $0.5 \le |Sk| < 1$ → moderately skewed; $|Sk| \ge 1$ → highly skewed.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** The weights (in kg) of 8 athletes are: 55, 60, 62, 58, 65, 70, 68, 72. Using Chebyshev's Theorem, find the minimum percentage of athletes whose weights lie between (i) 55.1 kg and 80.9 kg, and (ii) 47.6 kg and 88.4 kg.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the key steps involved in Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) with a suitable example. Mention at least four visualization techniques used in EDA.

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — Chebyshev's Theorem

**Step 1: Compute the Mean**
$$\bar{x} = \frac{55+60+62+58+65+70+68+72}{8} = \frac{510}{8} = 63.75$$

**Step 2: Compute Standard Deviation**

| $x_i$ | $x_i - 63.75$ | $(x_i - 63.75)^2$ |
|---|---|---|
| 55 | -8.75 | 76.5625 |
| 60 | -3.75 | 14.0625 |
| 62 | -1.75 | 3.0625 |
| 58 | -5.75 | 33.0625 |
| 65 | 1.25 | 1.5625 |
| 70 | 6.25 | 39.0625 |
| 68 | 4.25 | 18.0625 |
| 72 | 8.25 | 68.0625 |
| **Sum** | **0.00** | **253.5000** |

$$s^2 = \frac{253.50}{8-1} = 36.21, \quad s = \sqrt{36.21} = 6.018$$

**Step 3: Solve Part (i) — Range 55.1 to 80.9**
This range is $\bar{x} \pm k\sigma = 63.75 \pm 17.8$ kg. So:
$$k = \frac{17.8}{6.018} = 2.96 \approx 3$$

Applying Chebyshev's Theorem (using k = 2.96):
$$\text{Minimum \%} = \left(1 - \frac{1}{(2.96)^2}\right) \times 100\% = \left(1 - \frac{1}{8.76}\right) \times 100\%$$

$$= (1 - 0.1142) \times 100\% = 88.58\%$$

**Step 4: Solve Part (ii) — Range 47.6 to 88.4**
This range is $\bar{x} \pm 24.8$ kg. So:
$$k = \frac{24.8}{6.018} = 4.12$$

Applying Chebyshev's Theorem:
$$\text{Minimum \%} = \left(1 - \frac{1}{(4.12)^2}\right) \times 100\% = \left(1 - \frac{1}{16.97}\right) \times 100\%$$

$$= (1 - 0.0589) \times 100\% = 94.11\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** As k increases, Chebyshev's lower bound approaches 100% — but it's always a **minimum guarantee**, not the actual percentage. Use it when distribution shape is unknown.

---

#### Part (b) — Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)

**Step 1: Define EDA**
EDA is the preliminary investigation of a dataset using summary statistics and graphical visualizations to uncover patterns, anomalies, and relationships before formal hypothesis testing. It was popularized by **John Tukey** in 1977.

**Step 2: Key Steps in EDA**

1. **Data Collection & Initial Inspection** — Load the data, check dimensions, view the first few rows, identify variable types.
   *Example:* Inspecting a sales dataset with 10,000 rows and 15 columns — checking for missing values, data types (int, float, string).

2. **Handling Missing Data & Outliers** — Identify missing values (using `isnull()`), decide on imputation strategies, detect outliers using box plots or z-scores.
   *Example:* Filling missing product prices with the column median.

3. **Univariate Analysis** — Analyze each variable independently. Use histograms for continuous data, bar charts for categorical, box plots for spread, and compute mean/median/SD.
   *Example:* Histogram of customer ages showing right-skew.

4. **Bivariate/Multivariate Analysis** — Examine relationships between variables. Use scatter plots, correlation matrices, and crosstabs.
   *Example:* Scatter plot of advertising spend vs. sales showing a positive linear relationship.

5. **Pattern Discovery & Hypothesis Generation** — Summarize findings, note anomalies, and generate hypotheses for confirmatory analysis.
   *Example:* Noticing that sales drop sharply in July across multiple years — forming a hypothesis for seasonality.

**Step 3: Four Visualization Techniques in EDA**

| Technique | Purpose | Example Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Histogram** | Show distribution shape of continuous data | Customer age distribution |
| **Box Plot** | Show median, quartiles, outliers | Salary distribution across departments |
| **Scatter Plot** | Show relationship between two continuous variables | Height vs. weight |
| **Heatmap / Correlation Matrix** | Show pairwise correlations | Identifying multicollinearity in features |

**Additional techniques:** Bar charts (categorical counts), Q-Q plots (normality check), violin plots (distribution + density), pair plots (multi-variable scatter matrix).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final EDA Insight:** EDA is **iterative** — findings in one step often lead to re-examining earlier assumptions. It is a cyclical, not linear, process.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 1-2 Marks Each):**
> 1. **Mixing n and n-1:** Use n-1 in the **denominator for sample variance**. Using n gives a biased estimate and is a common error.
> 2. **Misapplying the Empirical Rule:** Never apply the 68-95-99.7 rule to skewed or unknown-shape data. Use Chebyshev's Theorem instead.
> 3. **Skipping units in answers:** Always mention units (kg, m, %, etc.) in the final answer — "5 marks" without units is marked down.
> 4. **Wrong interpolation in percentiles:** When L is fractional, do not round — interpolate using $x_{\lfloor L \rfloor} + \text{fraction} \times (x_{\lceil L \rceil} - x_{\lfloor L \rfloor})$.
> 5. **Confusing skewness signs:** Positive skew ≠ always good; it simply means right tail is longer. Don't add subjective value judgments.
> 6. **Forgetting to verify Σ(xi - x̄) = 0:** This sanity check catches arithmetic errors. KTU examiners reward verification.
> 7. **PMF vs PDF confusion:** PMF gives P(X = x) directly; PDF gives a *density* — probability is the *area under the curve over an interval*, not f(x) itself.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Quartiles** = 4 parts; **Deciles** = 10 parts; **Percentiles** = 100 parts. Q2 = D5 = P50 = Median.
- **Mean** is sensitive to outliers; **Median** is robust; **Mode** is for categorical data.
- **Sample variance** uses denominator **n-1** (Bessel's correction). **Population variance** uses **N**.
- **Skewness > 0** = right tail; **Skewness < 0** = left tail; **= 0** = symmetric.
- **Kurtosis = 3** = normal tails (mesokurtic); **> 3** = heavy tails (leptokurtic, e.g., t-distribution); **< 3** = light tails (platykurtic, e.g., uniform).
- **Empirical Rule** → only for bell-shaped data → 68-95-99.7.
- **Chebyshev's Theorem** → for ANY distribution → minimum $1 - 1/k^2$ fraction within kσ.
- **Outlier rule** (Tukey's fence): Values < Q1 − 1.5×IQR or > Q3 + 1.5×IQR are outliers.
- **EDA** is non-confirmatory — it explores, it does not prove. Use **histograms, box plots, scatter plots, Q-Q plots** as primary tools.
- **PMF** (discrete) sums to 1; **PDF** (continuous) integrates to 1.
- **For continuous distributions**, $P(X = x) = 0$; compute probability over **intervals**.
- **CV (Coefficient of Variation)** is the only dispersion measure that allows comparison across datasets with different units.
- **Always state assumptions** when applying the Empirical Rule (bell-shaped) or Chebyshev (k > 1).
- **Order of operations in EDA:** Clean → Describe → Visualize → Detect Outliers → Hypothesize.
- **KTU golden rule for writing answers:** Show the formula → substitute values → compute step-by-step → state the final answer with units → interpret the result.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Samples and Population
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET501 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Samples and Population

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Population — The Complete Universe of Interest

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Population** in statistics is the *entire* set of individuals, objects, events, or measurements that share at least one common observable characteristic and form the subject of a statistical investigation. It is also called the **Universe** or **Target Population**.

In the KTU 2024 Data Analytics framework, a population is *not* just a count of people. It can be:

- A set of all transactions in a banking system.
- The complete set of IoT sensor readings in a smart city for one year.
- All pixels in a high-resolution satellite image.
- Every possible outcome of a machine learning model's prediction.

### Key Population Terminology

| Term | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Finite Population** | A population with a fixed, countable size $N$ | 5,000 students in a Kerala engineering college |
| **Infinite Population** | A population whose size is unbounded or uncountable | All possible throws of a fair die |
| **Hypothetical Population** | A conceptual population derived from a theoretical process | Distribution of measurement errors in a sensor |
| **Sampling Frame** | The actual, practical list from which the sample is drawn | College roll list for the 5,000 students |
| **Parameter** | A numerical summary of the population (Greek letters) | Population mean $\mu$, population variance $\sigma^2$ |

---

## 1.2 Sample — A Representative Subset

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Sample** is a finite subset of individuals or observations selected from the population according to a well-defined rule (the *sampling procedure*). A numerical summary computed from a sample is called a **Statistic**.

A **statistic** is to a sample what a **parameter** is to a population:

$$\text{Population} \rightarrow \text{Parameter} \;(\mu, \sigma^2, p) \qquad \qquad \text{Sample} \rightarrow \text{Statistic} \;(\bar{x}, s^2, \hat{p})$$

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Soup Tasting" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Soup Tasting Method**
> Imagine a chef has prepared a huge cauldron of soup for a wedding. The chef cannot possibly taste the *entire* cauldron to test its saltiness. So, the chef stirs the soup well, dips a small ladle, and tastes a spoonful. From that one spoonful, the chef infers the saltiness of the whole cauldron.

In this analogy:

- **The Cauldron** = The **Population** (all the soup = all data points).
- **The Spoonful** = The **Sample** (a small, carefully selected subset).
- **The Stirring** = A good **random sampling** technique (ensures every part is equally likely to be chosen).
- **The Chef's Taste Judgment** = The **Statistic / Inference** (an estimate of the true population saltiness = parameter).

The quality of the chef's judgment depends entirely on **how well the spoonful represents the whole cauldron**. If the chef only tasted from the top (a *biased* sample), the judgment would be wrong.

---

## 1.4 Why Sampling? The Data Analytics Reality

In Data Analytics and real-world engineering, it is almost always **impossible, impractical, or destructive** to study an entire population:

1. **Cost** — Testing every smartphone battery manufactured in a year would bankrupt the company.
2. **Time** — A full census of India's population takes years; we need insights in days.
3. **Destructiveness** — Testing a light bulb *destroys* it, so we test a sample.
4. **Infinite Populations** — The stream of credit card transactions never ends.
5. **Accuracy vs Precision Trade-off** — A well-designed sample is often *more accurate* than a poorly-conducted full census, because more resources can be devoted per unit of inspection.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Insight:**
> In ESE answers, when asked "Why sampling?", always list *at least three* of the above reasons. A single-sentence answer "because population is large" will fetch only partial marks.

---

## 1.5 The Sampling Process — A High-Level Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> **The 5-Stage KTU Sampling Pipeline:**
> 1. **Define the Target Population** clearly and unambiguously.
> 2. **Identify the Sampling Frame** (the practical list you can actually sample from).
> 3. **Choose a Sampling Technique** (random, stratified, cluster, etc.).
> 4. **Determine Sample Size $n$** (using statistical formulas).
> 5. **Draw the Sample** and compute statistics, then infer population parameters.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Census vs Sample — A Side-by-Side Comparison

| Aspect | Census (Full Enumeration) | Sample Survey |
|---|---|---|
| **Coverage** | Every unit in the population | A carefully chosen subset |
| **Cost** | Very high | Manageable |
| **Time** | Long | Short |
| **Accuracy of each unit** | Often rushed | High-quality per unit |
| **Sampling Error** | None (but non-sampling errors exist) | Present, but quantifiable |
| **Suitability for large populations** | Impractical | Ideal |
| **Result Type** | Parameter (exact) | Statistic (estimate) |

---

## 2.2 Parameters vs Statistics — The Formal Mapping

| Concept | Population (Parameter) | Sample (Statistic) |
|---|---|---|
| **Size** | $N$ | $n$ |
| **Mean** | $\mu = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i}{N}$ | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}{n}$ |
| **Variance** | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \mu)^2}{N}$ | $s^2 = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}$ |
| **Std. Deviation** | $\sigma$ | $s$ |
| **Proportion** | $P$ | $\hat{p}$ |
| **Notation Style** | Greek letters | Latin letters |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $n-1$ in the sample variance?**
> This is **Bessel's Correction**. Because the sample mean $\bar{x}$ itself is an estimate of $\mu$, we have lost one degree of freedom. Using $n-1$ makes $s^2$ an **unbiased estimator** of $\sigma^2$ — that is, $E[s^2] = \sigma^2$.

---

## 2.3 Types of Sampling Techniques — The KTU Master Classification

### A. Probability Sampling (Every unit has a known, non-zero chance of selection)

#### 1. Simple Random Sampling (SRS)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Every possible sample of size $n$ has an **equal probability** of being selected from the population of size $N$.

- **With Replacement (SRSWR):** Total possible samples = $N^n$.
- **Without Replacement (SRSWOR):** Total possible samples = $\binom{N}{n} = \dfrac{N!}{n!(N-n)!}$.

Variance of sample mean under SRSWOR:
$$\text{Var}(\bar{x}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n} \left( \frac{N-n}{N-1} \right)$$

The term $\dfrac{N-n}{N-1}$ is called the **Finite Population Correction (FPC)** factor. When $n \le 0.05 N$, the FPC $\approx 1$ and the formula reduces to $\sigma^2 / n$.

#### 2. Systematic Sampling

- Pick every $k$-th element from a randomly chosen starting point, where $k = \lfloor N/n \rfloor$.
- Risk: **Periodicity** — if the population has a hidden cycle, results are biased.
- Variance estimator: $\hat{\text{Var}}(\bar{x}_{sy}) = \dfrac{s^2}{n} \left( 1 - \dfrac{n}{N} \right)$ where $s^2$ is the variance of the $n$ selected units.

#### 3. Stratified Sampling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The population is divided into mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive **strata** (homogeneous sub-groups), and a random sample is drawn from each stratum.

- **Proportional Allocation:** $n_h = n \cdot \dfrac{N_h}{N}$.
- **Neyman (Optimal) Allocation:** $n_h = n \cdot \dfrac{N_h \sigma_h}{\sum N_h \sigma_h}$.

The stratified mean estimator is:
$$\bar{x}_{st} = \sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h \bar{x}_h$$
where $W_h = N_h / N$ is the stratum weight and $L$ is the number of strata.

**Advantage:** Strata are chosen to be *internally homogeneous* but *externally heterogeneous* — this reduces variance dramatically.

#### 4. Cluster Sampling

- The population is divided into **clusters** (typically geographic or natural groups). Some clusters are randomly selected and *all* elements in those clusters are studied.
- **Used when:** A complete sampling frame is unavailable, but cluster boundaries are easy to identify (e.g., villages, schools, factory shifts).
- Increases variance compared to SRS but is **logistically cheap**.

### B. Non-Probability Sampling (Selection is based on convenience/judgment)

| Method | How it Works | Risk |
|---|---|---|
| **Convenience Sampling** | Whoever is easiest to reach | High selection bias |
| **Judgmental/Purposive** | Researcher hand-picks "typical" cases | Researcher bias |
| **Snowball Sampling** | Existing subjects recruit future subjects | Network bias |
| **Quota Sampling** | Pre-set quotas for sub-groups | Selection bias |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Non-probability samples **cannot** be used to make generalizable statistical inferences (confidence intervals, hypothesis tests). Examiners often deduct marks for confusing *quota sampling* with *stratified sampling* — they are **fundamentally different**.

---

## 2.4 Sampling Error vs Non-Sampling Error

| Error Type | Definition | Source | Can be reduced by |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sampling Error** | Difference between sample statistic and true population parameter | Natural variation between samples | Increasing $n$, better design |
| **Non-Sampling Error** | Errors in data collection, recording, response | Interviewer bias, faulty instruments, non-response | Better training, calibration |

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial KTU Fact:** Sampling error is **inherent** to any sample-based study and can be *quantified* but never *eliminated*. Non-sampling error can be larger in magnitude but is often overlooked.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this table — it appears in nearly every Part A and Part B question on this module.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sample Mean | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum x_i}{n}$ | Always |
| 2 | Sample Variance (unbiased) | $s^2 = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}$ | Always |
| 3 | Std. Error of Mean (SRSWR) | $SE(\bar{x}) = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | $n \le 0.05 N$ |
| 4 | Std. Error of Mean (SRSWOR) | $SE(\bar{x}) = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \sqrt{\dfrac{N-n}{N-1}}$ | $n > 0.05 N$ |
| 5 | Sample Size for Mean | $n = \left( \dfrac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E} \right)^2$ | Estimating $\mu$ within margin $E$ |
| 6 | Sample Size for Proportion | $n = \dfrac{z_{\alpha/2}^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{E^2}$ | Estimating $p$ within margin $E$ |
| 7 | Finite Population Correction | $FPC = \sqrt{\dfrac{N-n}{N-1}}$ | When $n/N > 0.05$ |
| 8 | Sample size in Stratified (Prop.) | $n_h = n \cdot \dfrac{N_h}{N}$ | Proportional allocation |
| 9 | Sample size in Stratified (Neyman) | $n_h = n \cdot \dfrac{N_h \sigma_h}{\sum N_h \sigma_h}$ | Optimal allocation |
| 10 | Sampling Interval (Systematic) | $k = \lfloor N/n \rfloor$ | Systematic sampling |

> **Notation in formulas:** $z_{\alpha/2}$ is the critical value from the standard normal distribution for confidence level $(1-\alpha)$. Common values: $z_{0.05} = 1.96$ (95% CI), $z_{0.01} = 2.576$ (99% CI), $z_{0.10} = 1.645$ (90% CI).

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing Quality Control:** Sampling batches for defect-rate estimation.
- **Network Engineering:** Packet sampling for traffic analysis (sFlow, NetFlow).
- **Medical Trials:** Stratified sampling ensures drug trials cover all age groups.
- **A/B Testing in Web Analytics:** Random sampling of users to test UI changes.
- **IoT Sensor Networks:** Cluster sampling of sensor nodes to estimate city-wide pollution.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Sample Size Formula for Estimating a Population Mean

**Problem Setup:** You want to estimate a population mean $\mu$ using sample mean $\bar{x}$ with a specified **margin of error** $E$ at confidence level $(1-\alpha)$.

**Starting Point:** The confidence interval for $\mu$ under SRS:
$$\bar{x} - E \le \mu \le \bar{x} + E \quad \text{where} \quad E = z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Step-by-step derivation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Write the margin of error definition} \quad & E = z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2: Divide both sides by } E \quad & \frac{E}{E} = \frac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E \sqrt{n}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 = \frac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E \sqrt{n}} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3: Multiply both sides by } \sqrt{n} \quad & \sqrt{n} = \frac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 4: Square both sides} \quad & n = \left( \frac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E} \right)^2 \\[6pt]
\text{Step 5: Expand} \quad & n = \frac{z_{\alpha/2}^2 \cdot \sigma^2}{E^2}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\,n = \left( \dfrac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E} \right)^{2}\,}$$

> **Logic Note:** This formula tells us — to halve the margin of error $E$, we must **quadruple** the sample size. That is the fundamental cost-vs-precision trade-off in Data Analytics.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Sample Size Formula for Estimating a Proportion

**Setup:** Estimating a population proportion $P$ with margin of error $E$ at confidence level $(1-\alpha)$. Sample proportion $\hat{p}$ is used, and $\text{Var}(\hat{p}) = \dfrac{P(1-P)}{n}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Margin of error} \quad & E = z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{P(1-P)}{n}} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2: Solve for } n \quad & \sqrt{n} = \frac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sqrt{P(1-P)}}{E} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3: Square} \quad & n = \frac{z_{\alpha/2}^{2} \cdot P(1-P)}{E^{2}}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\,n = \dfrac{z_{\alpha/2}^{2} \cdot P(1-P)}{E^{2}}\,}$$

> **Logic Note:** $P(1-P)$ is maximized at $P = 0.5$, giving $P(1-P) = 0.25$. When $P$ is unknown, a common KTU convention is to **conservatively use $P = 0.5$** to obtain the largest (safest) sample size.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — SRS Sample Mean Variance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Style Question (2023 Pattern):**
> A population has $N = 500$ units with $\sigma^2 = 100$. A simple random sample of size $n = 50$ is drawn **without replacement**. Find the standard error of the sample mean.

**Step-by-step solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:} \quad & N = 500, \quad \sigma^2 = 100, \quad n = 50 \\[4pt]
\text{Step 1: Check if FPC matters} \quad & \frac{n}{N} = \frac{50}{500} = 0.10 > 0.05 \\[4pt]
& \Rightarrow \text{FPC must be applied} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2: Write the SRSWOR variance formula} \quad & \text{Var}(\bar{x}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n} \left( \frac{N-n}{N-1} \right) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3: Substitute values} \quad & \text{Var}(\bar{x}) = \frac{100}{50} \left( \frac{500 - 50}{500 - 1} \right) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4: Compute the FPC} \quad & \frac{450}{499} = 0.9018 \\[4pt]
\text{Step 5: Multiply} \quad & \text{Var}(\bar{x}) = 2 \times 0.9018 = 1.8036 \\[4pt]
\text{Step 6: Standard error} \quad & SE(\bar{x}) = \sqrt{1.8036} = 1.343
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\,SE(\bar{x}) = 1.343\,}$$

> **Interpretation:** A typical sample mean will deviate from the true population mean by about ±1.34 units. A 95% confidence interval would be $\bar{x} \pm 1.96 \times 1.343$.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Determining Sample Size

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Style Question (2024 Pattern):**
> A KTU researcher wants to estimate the mean CGPA of B.Tech students in Kerala within $E = 0.2$ points at 95% confidence. Past studies suggest $\sigma = 0.8$. Find the required sample size.

**Step-by-step solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:} \quad & E = 0.2, \quad \sigma = 0.8, \quad 1 - \alpha = 0.95 \\[4pt]
\text{Step 1: Identify } z_{\alpha/2} \quad & z_{0.025} = 1.96 \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2: Apply the formula} \quad & n = \left( \frac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E} \right)^2 \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3: Substitute} \quad & n = \left( \frac{1.96 \times 0.8}{0.2} \right)^2 \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4: Compute numerator} \quad & 1.96 \times 0.8 = 1.568 \\[4pt]
\text{Step 5: Divide by E} \quad & \frac{1.568}{0.2} = 7.84 \\[4pt]
\text{Step 6: Square} \quad & n = (7.84)^2 = 61.4656 \\[4pt]
\text{Step 7: Round UP} \quad & n = 62
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\,n = 62 \text{ students}\,}$$

> **KTU Note:** Always **round UP** when computing sample sizes. Rounding down would give a sample whose actual margin of error exceeds $E$, violating the design requirement.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Sampling Technique

| Scenario | Recommended Technique | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Homogeneous population, frame available | **Simple Random Sampling** | Easiest to implement, unbiased |
| Population has clear sub-groups (e.g., year of study) | **Stratified Sampling** | Guarantees representation, lower variance |
| Population is geographically spread, no central frame | **Cluster Sampling** | Reduces travel/logistic cost |
| Ordered list, no hidden periodicity | **Systematic Sampling** | Quick and easy |
| Exploratory pilot study, time is critical | **Convenience Sampling** | Fastest, but biased — *not for inference* |
| Hard-to-reach population (e.g., drug users) | **Snowball Sampling** | Uses social network to find cases |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Sampling Workflow — End-to-End Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["STEP 1: Define Target Population"] --> B["STEP 2: Identify Sampling Frame"]
    B --> C{"STEP 3: Is frame complete?"}
    C -- Yes --> D["STEP 4: Choose Sampling Technique"]
    C -- No --> E["STEP 4 alt: Use Cluster or Snowball"]
    D --> F["STEP 5: Determine Sample Size n"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["STEP 6: Draw Sample"]
    G --> H["STEP 7: Compute Statistic x-bar s2 p-hat"]
    H --> I["STEP 8: Estimate Parameter and Report Margin of Error"]
    I --> J["STEP 9: Decision: Accept Estimate or Resample"]
    J -- Resample --> F
    J -- Accept --> K["STEP 10: Final Inference Reported"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This flow shows the iterative KTU-recommended process. The diamond at Step 3 captures whether a complete sampling frame exists — a critical decision point that determines the technique family.

---

## 4.2 Classification Tree of Sampling Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["SAMPLING TECHNIQUES"] --> P["Probability Sampling"]
    S --> NP["Non-Probability Sampling"]
    P --> P1["Simple Random Sampling"]
    P --> P2["Systematic Sampling"]
    P --> P3["Stratified Sampling"]
    P --> P4["Cluster Sampling"]
    P3 --> P3A["Proportional Allocation"]
    P3 --> P3B["Neyman Optimal Allocation"]
    NP --> NP1["Convenience Sampling"]
    NP --> NP2["Judgmental Sampling"]
    NP --> NP3["Quota Sampling"]
    NP --> NP4["Snowball Sampling"]
```

> **Key takeaway:** The classification is **strictly binary** — every sampling method is either *probability* or *non-probability*. Mixing the two is a common KTU answer error.

---

## 4.3 Relationship Between Population, Sample, Parameter, and Statistic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    POP["POPULATION N units"] -->|summarized by| PAR["PARAMETER mu sigma2 P"]
    POP -->|draw n units| SAM["SAMPLE n units"]
    SAM -->|summarized by| STAT["STATISTIC x-bar s2 p-hat"]
    STAT -->|inference| PAR
    PAR -.->|true unknown value| INF["INFERENCE TARGET"]
    STAT -.->|best estimate| INF
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The solid arrows show the *direction of computation* (parameter from population, statistic from sample). The dashed arrows show the *inferential goal* — using the statistic to estimate the parameter.

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture: Standard Error Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN["Input: N, n, sigma"] --> CK{"n slash N greater than 0.05?"}
    CK -- Yes --> F["Apply Finite Population Correction"]
    CK -- No --> SF["Ignore FPC, use simple formula"]
    F --> CV["Compute Var x-bar = sigma2 slash n times N-n over N-1"]
    SF --> CV
    CV --> SE["SE x-bar = square root of Var x-bar"]
    SE --> OUT["Output: Standard Error"]
```

> **Engineering utility:** This is the same logical pipeline used in survey-software tools like R's `survey` package, Python's `statsmodels`, and SAS's `SURVEYMEANS` procedure.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Short-Answer Conceptual
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024 Model]`

**Q1. Differentiate between a population and a sample. Why is sampling preferred over a census in most data analytics studies?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Population | Sample |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | The entire group of interest | A subset drawn from the population |
| **Size** | $N$ (often very large or infinite) | $n$ (manageable size) |
| **Summary measure** | Parameter ($\mu$, $\sigma^2$) | Statistic ($\bar{x}$, $s^2$) |

**Reasons sampling is preferred over a census (3 of 5 needed for full marks):**
1. **Lower cost** — fewer resources required for collection and processing.
2. **Faster results** — sampling takes days, census takes years.
3. **Higher per-unit accuracy** — trained staff can focus quality on fewer units.
4. **Practical for infinite populations** — e.g., continuous manufacturing, streaming data.
5. **Less destructive** — e.g., testing light bulbs, crash-testing cars.

*(3 marks: 1 for distinction table, 2 for listing at least 3 reasons.)*

---

### Question 2: Short-Answer Definitional
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model]`

**Q2. Define the Finite Population Correction (FPC). Under what condition is it safe to ignore the FPC in standard error calculations?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Finite Population Correction (FPC)** is a multiplicative factor applied to the variance of a sample statistic when sampling is done **without replacement** from a finite population. It accounts for the fact that, after each draw, the population effectively shrinks.

$$FPC = \sqrt{\frac{N-n}{N-1}}$$

The full SRSWOR variance becomes:
$$\text{Var}(\bar{x}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n} \cdot \frac{N-n}{N-1}$$

**Condition to ignore FPC:** When the sampling fraction $n/N \le 0.05$ (i.e., 5%), the FPC $\approx 1$ and its effect on variance is negligible. In this case, one may use the simpler formula $\text{Var}(\bar{x}) \approx \sigma^2 / n$.

*(3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for definition, 1 for the 5% rule.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Choice A — Full Solution

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**Question A(a) [7 Marks]:**
**"Explain the different probability sampling techniques with suitable examples. Compare Simple Random Sampling and Stratified Sampling in terms of variance reduction."**

**Model Answer:**

**Probability Sampling Techniques:**

1. **Simple Random Sampling (SRS):** Every unit in the population has an equal probability of selection. *Example:* Picking 50 students from a roll of 1000 using a random number table.

2. **Systematic Sampling:** Select every $k$-th unit from a randomly chosen start. *Example:* Inspecting every 10th product on an assembly line.

3. **Stratified Sampling:** Population is divided into homogeneous strata; random sample is drawn from each. *Example:* Sampling students separately from each department.

4. **Cluster Sampling:** Population is divided into clusters; whole clusters are randomly selected. *Example:* Selecting 5 villages out of 50 and surveying all households in those villages.

**Variance Comparison: SRS vs Stratified**

Under proportional allocation, the variance of the stratified sample mean is:
$$\text{Var}(\bar{x}_{st}) = \sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h^2 \cdot \frac{\sigma_h^2}{n_h} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h \sigma_h^2$$

Variance of SRS sample mean: $\text{Var}(\bar{x}_{SRS}) = \sigma^2 / n$

The key result is:
$$\text{Var}(\bar{x}_{st}) = \text{Var}(\bar{x}_{SRS}) - \frac{1}{n} \sum W_h(\mu_h - \mu)^2$$

The second term is **always non-negative**, meaning stratified sampling **always has equal or lower variance** than SRS. The reduction is greatest when **stratum means are very different** (high between-stratum variability) and **within-stratum variance is small** (homogeneous strata).

*(7 marks: 4 for techniques, 3 for variance comparison derivation and interpretation.)*

---

**Question A(b) [7 Marks]:**
**"A researcher wants to estimate the mean lifetime of a new LED bulb model. Past data gives $\sigma = 400$ hours. How many bulbs must be tested to ensure the margin of error is at most 50 hours at 99% confidence? If the same study is repeated for a finite production batch of $N = 2000$ bulbs, what is the adjusted sample size?"**

**Model Answer:**

**Part (b1): Infinite Population Case**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:} \quad & E = 50, \quad \sigma = 400, \quad 1 - \alpha = 0.99 \\[4pt]
\text{Critical value:} \quad & z_{0.005} = 2.576 \\[4pt]
\text{Apply formula:} \quad & n_0 = \left( \frac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{2.576 \times 400}{50} \right)^2 \\[4pt]
& = \left( \frac{1030.4}{50} \right)^2 = (20.608)^2 \\[4pt]
& n_0 = 424.69 \approx 425 \text{ bulbs}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b2: Finite Population with $N = 2000$)**

Apply the finite population correction to the sample size:

$$\begin{aligned}
n & = \frac{n_0}{1 + \frac{n_0 - 1}{N}} = \frac{425}{1 + \frac{424}{2000}} \\[4pt]
& = \frac{425}{1 + 0.212} = \frac{425}{1.212} \\[4pt]
& n = 350.66 \approx 351 \text{ bulbs}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\,n_{\infty} = 425, \quad n_{\text{finite}} = 351\,}$$

> **Note on the formula:** $n = \dfrac{n_0 N}{N + n_0 - 1}$ is the KTU-preferred form for the finite-population-adjusted sample size.

*(7 marks: 3 for $n_0$, 2 for finite adjustment, 2 for the final answer with rounding.)*

---

### Choice B — Full Solution (Alternative)

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Question B(a) [7 Marks]:**
**"Explain the concepts of sampling error and non-sampling error. A population has $N = 10000$ with $\sigma = 25$. Two SRSWOR samples of size $n = 100$ and $n = 400$ are drawn. Compare the standard errors and interpret the result."**

**Model Answer:**

**Sampling Error:** The natural variability between a sample statistic and the true population parameter, caused by the fact that we observe only a subset. It is **quantifiable** and **reduces with larger $n$**.

**Non-Sampling Error:** Errors arising from data collection problems — interviewer bias, faulty measurement, non-response, recording mistakes. They are **not quantifiable** in a simple formula and **do not necessarily reduce** with larger $n$.

**Standard Error Comparison:**

For SRSWOR: $SE(\bar{x}) = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \sqrt{\dfrac{N-n}{N-1}}$

**Sample 1: $n = 100$**

$$\begin{aligned}
FPC_1 & = \sqrt{\frac{10000 - 100}{10000 - 1}} = \sqrt{\frac{9900}{9999}} = \sqrt{0.9901} = 0.9950 \\[4pt]
SE_1 & = \frac{25}{\sqrt{100}} \times 0.9950 = 2.5 \times 0.9950 = 2.4875
\end{aligned}$$

**Sample 2: $n = 400$**

$$\begin{aligned}
FPC_2 & = \sqrt{\frac{10000 - 400}{10000 - 1}} = \sqrt{\frac{9600}{9999}} = \sqrt{0.9601} = 0.9798 \\[4pt]
SE_2 & = \frac{25}{\sqrt{400}} \times 0.9798 = 1.25 \times 0.9798 = 1.2248
\end{aligned}$$

**Comparison and Interpretation:**

$$\frac{SE_1}{SE_2} = \frac{2.4875}{1.2248} \approx 2.03$$

Quadrupling the sample size ($100 \rightarrow 400$) reduces the standard error by a factor of approximately 2. This confirms the theoretical square-root law: $SE \propto 1/\sqrt{n}$. To halve the standard error, the sample size must be quadrupled.

*(7 marks: 2 for definitions, 2 for $SE_1$, 2 for $SE_2$, 1 for interpretation.)*

---

**Question B(b) [7 Marks]:**
**"Describe Cluster Sampling. A city has 50 wards, each containing 200 households. To estimate the average monthly electricity bill, the city council selects 5 wards at random and surveys every household in those wards. Estimate the mean bill and the standard error if the sample gives: total households surveyed = 1000, $\sum x_i = 250000$, $\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 = 6250000$."**

**Model Answer:**

**Cluster Sampling Description:**

In cluster sampling, the population is divided into naturally occurring groups (**clusters**), and a random sample of clusters is selected. *All* units within the chosen clusters are observed. This is efficient when:
- A complete frame of individual units is unavailable.
- Clusters are geographically dispersed (reduces travel cost).
- Within-cluster homogeneity is acceptable (i.e., between-cluster variance is what matters).

The mean estimator is:
$$\bar{x}_{clu} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \bar{x}_i$$

where $\bar{x}_i$ is the mean of the $i$-th sampled cluster.

**Numerical Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:} \quad & \text{Wards (clusters)} = 50, \quad \text{Sampled wards} = 5 \\[4pt]
& \text{Households per ward} = 200, \quad \text{Total sampled} = 1000 \\[4pt]
& \sum x_i = 250000, \quad \sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2 = 6250000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 1: Sample mean**
$$\bar{x} = \frac{250000}{1000} = 250 \text{ rupees}$$

**Step 2: Sample variance**
$$s^2 = \frac{6250000}{1000 - 1} = \frac{6250000}{999} = 6256.26$$

**Step 3: Standard Error of cluster mean**

For equal-sized clusters of size $M = 200$ with $n = 5$ clusters sampled from $N = 50$:
$$SE(\bar{x}_{clu}) = \sqrt{\frac{N-n}{N \cdot n} \cdot \frac{\sum(\bar{x}_i - \bar{x}_{clu})^2}{n-1}}$$

Approximating the between-cluster variance using the within-sample variance (with the simplifying assumption):
$$SE \approx \sqrt{\frac{s^2}{n}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{N-n}{N}} = \sqrt{\frac{6256.26}{5}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{45}{50}}$$

$$SE \approx \sqrt{1251.25} \cdot \sqrt{0.9} = 35.37 \times 0.949 = 33.56$$

$$\boxed{\,\bar{x} = \text{Rs. } 250, \quad SE \approx \text{Rs. } 33.56\,}$$

> **95% CI for the true mean bill:** $250 \pm 1.96 \times 33.56 = 250 \pm 65.78 \Rightarrow$ (Rs. 184.22, Rs. 315.78)

*(7 marks: 2 for cluster sampling description, 2 for $\bar{x}$, 1 for $s^2$, 2 for SE and CI.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls on this Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "statistic" and "parameter"** — sample quantities use $\bar{x}, s, \hat{p}$ (Latin); population uses $\mu, \sigma, P$ (Greek). Mixing these is an instant 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting to round UP** in sample-size calculations — rounding *down* invalidates the design.
> 3. **Omitting the FPC** when $n/N > 0.05$ — examiners specifically check this.
> 4. **Confusing stratified with quota sampling** — stratified is *probability*; quota is *non-probability*.
> 5. **Writing $s^2 = \frac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n}$** — this is the *biased* (ML) estimator. KTU expects the *unbiased* $n-1$ version unless explicitly told otherwise.
> 6. **Failing to interpret the answer** — always close with one sentence: "Thus, a 95% CI for $\mu$ is..." Examiners reward interpretation marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Samples and Population":**
>
> - **Population** = entire group; **Sample** = subset. *Parameter* describes population, *Statistic* describes sample.
> - Greek letters ($\mu, \sigma^2, P$) for parameters; Latin letters ($\bar{x}, s^2, \hat{p}$) for statistics.
> - $s^2$ uses $n-1$ in the denominator (Bessel's correction for unbiasedness).
> - **Five probability techniques:** SRS, Systematic, Stratified, Cluster, Multistage.
> - **Four non-probability techniques:** Convenience, Judgmental, Quota, Snowball.
> - **Sampling error** = random deviation, reducible by larger $n$. **Non-sampling error** = human/systematic, not reduced by $n$.
> - **FPC** must be applied when $n/N > 0.05$.
> - **Sample size for mean:** $n = \left(\dfrac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^2$ — *always round up*.
> - **Sample size for proportion:** $n = \dfrac{z_{\alpha/2}^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{E^2}$ — use $p=0.5$ conservatively.
> - **Stratified sampling** always has equal-or-lower variance than SRS.
> - **Systematic sampling** is fast but vulnerable to periodicity bias.
> - **Cluster sampling** is cheap but increases variance — only efficient when between-cluster variance is high.
> - $SE \propto 1/\sqrt{n}$ — to halve the SE, quadruple the sample size.
> - **In KTU answers, always show the formula → substitute → compute → interpret pipeline.**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
