**Conclusion:** The sampling fraction is approximately 4.92%, which is just below the 5% threshold. If the institution uses the 5% rule, $n_0 = 246$ is acceptable. If FPC is conservatively applied, the adjusted sample size is **235 smartphones**. The reduction is only 11 units (~4.5%) because the sampling fraction is small.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q (a)** A marketing analyst wants to estimate the proportion of customers in Kerala who prefer online shopping over in-store shopping. There is no prior estimate of this proportion. She desires a 99% confidence level and a margin of error of $E = 0.04$. Calculate the required sample size. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** Two competing machine learning models (Model A and Model B) are being compared for classification accuracy. Model A has an accuracy of 75% and Model B has 82%. The analyst wants to detect this difference with 95% confidence ($\alpha = 0.05$) and 80% power ($Z_{\beta} = 0.84$). Assuming equal sample sizes in both groups, calculate the required sample size per group. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Sample Size for Proportion (Conservative Case):**

**Step 1:** Identify the parameters:
- No prior estimate of $p$ $\Rightarrow$ use the **conservative value** $p = 0.5$, giving $p(1-p) = 0.25$
- Confidence level: $99\%$, so $Z_{\alpha/2} = 2.576$
- Margin of error: $E = 0.04$

**Step 2:** Apply the proportion sample size formula:

$$n_0 = \frac{Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot p(1-p)}{E^{2}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the values:

$$n_0 = \frac{(2.576)^{2} \times 0.25}{(0.04)^{2}}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the numerator:

$$(2.576)^{2} = 6.635776$$

$$6.635776 \times 0.25 = 1.658944$$

**Step 5:** Compute the denominator:

$$(0.04)^{2} = 0.0016$$

**Step 6:** Divide:

$$n_0 = \frac{1.658944}{0.0016} = 1036.84$$

**Step 7:** Round UP to the next integer:

$$\boxed{n_0 = 1{,}037 \text{ customers}}$$

**Part (b) — Sample Size for Two-Proportion Hypothesis Test:**

**Step 1:** Identify the parameters:
- $p_1 = 0.75$ (Model A), so $q_1 = 1 - 0.75 = 0.25$
- $p_2 = 0.82$ (Model B), so $q_2 = 1 - 0.82 = 0.18$
- $Z_{\alpha/2} = 1.96$ (for 95% confidence, two-tailed)
- $Z_{\beta} = 0.84$ (for 80% power)

**Step 2:** Apply the two-proportion formula:

$$n = \frac{(Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^{2} \cdot [p_1(1-p_1) + p_2(1-p_2)]}{(p_1 - p_2)^{2}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the sum of Z-values:

$$Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta} = 1.96 + 0.84 = 2.80$$

**Step 4:** Square the sum:

$$(2.80)^{2} = 7.84$$

**Step 5:** Compute the variance bracket:

$$p_1(1-p_1) + p_2(1-p_2) = (0.75 \times 0.25) + (0.82 \times 0.18)$$

$$= 0.1875 + 0.1476 = 0.3351$$

**Step 6:** Compute the squared difference:

$$(p_1 - p_2)^{2} = (0.75 - 0.82)^{2} = (-0.07)^{2} = 0.0049$$

**Step 7:** Multiply the numerator:

$$7.84 \times 0.3351 = 2.627184$$

**Step 8:** Divide by the denominator:

$$n = \frac{2.627184}{0.0049} \approx 536.16$$

**Step 9:** Round UP:

$$\boxed{n = 537 \text{ samples per group}}$$

**Conclusion:** The marketing analyst must survey **1,037 customers** to estimate the online shopping preference, and the ML comparison study requires **537 test cases per model** (1,074 total) to detect the 7% accuracy difference with 95% confidence and 80% power.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Master These Before the Exam:**

- [x] **Core Philosophy:** SSD inverts the confidence interval formula — *specify* the desired error and confidence, *compute* the sample size.
- [x] **Mean Formula:** $n = (Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma / E)^{2}$ — use when variable is **continuous**.
- [x] **Proportion Formula:** $n = Z_{\alpha/2}^{2} \cdot p(1-p) / E^{2}$ — use when variable is **binary**.
- [x] **Conservative $p$:** Always use $p = 0.5$ when no prior estimate exists; it maximizes variance and guarantees precision.
- [x] **FPC Threshold:** Apply Finite Population Correction when $n_0 / N > 0.05$ (the "5% rule").
- [x] **FPC Formula:** $n_{adj} = n_0 / [1 + (n_0 - 1)/N]$ — always gives a smaller $n$ than $n_0$.
- [x] **Two-Sample Test:** Formula uses $(Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^{2}$ — note the **sum** of Z-values, not the product.
- [x] **Critical Z-Values to Memorize:** 1.645 (90%), **1.96 (95%)**, 2.576 (99%), 0.84 (80% power).
- [x] **Round UP Rule:** Always round the final sample size UP. Never round down — it violates the margin of error guarantee.
- [x] **Quadratic Scaling:** $n$ scales as $1/E^{2}$ — halving $E$ quadruples $n$.
- [x] **Quadratic Variability:** $n$ scales as $\sigma^{2}$ — doubling $\sigma$ quadruples $n$.
- [x] **Proportion Range:** $p(1-p)$ is maximized at $p=0.5$ (value 0.25) and minimized at $p=0$ or $p=1$ (value 0).
- [x] **Power $1-\beta$:** Higher power requires larger $n$; $Z_{\beta} = 0.84$ corresponds to 80% power.
- [x] **Two-Tailed vs. One-Tailed:** KTU problems use **two-tailed** confidence intervals (so $Z_{\alpha/2}$) unless explicitly stated.
- [x] **Units Check:** $E$ and $\sigma$ must be in the **same units** (e.g., both in hours, both in mmHg, both as decimals for proportions).
- [x] **Engineering Relevance:** SSD is foundational for A/B testing, quality control, clinical trials, and survey design — the *single most important* inferential statistics skill for any data analyst.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Sampling Distributions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET501 - DATA ANALYTICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Sampling Distributions # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sampling Distribution (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)** > A **Sampling Distribution** is a probability distribution of a statistic (such as the sample mean, sample proportion, or sample variance) that is obtained from a large number of independent random samples drawn from the same underlying population. It describes how the statistic would vary from one sample to another purely due to random sampling variation. In the context of KTU's **Data Analytics** framework, the sampling distribution forms the **theoretical bridge** between descriptive sample statistics and the unknown population parameters we seek to infer. It is the cornerstone of all inferential statistics. ### Key Terminology | Term | Definition | |------|------------| | **Population (N)** | The entire set of observations or units under study. | | **Sample (n)** | A subset of the population, of size $n$, actually observed. | | **Parameter** | A numerical characteristic of the **population** (e.g., $\mu, \sigma^2, p$). | | **Statistic** | A numerical characteristic computed **from the sample** (e.g., $\bar{X}, s^2, \hat{p}$). | | **Standard Error (SE)** | The standard deviation of the sampling distribution of a statistic. | | **Sampling Distribution** | The probability distribution of a sample statistic over all possible samples of size $n$. | --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The Factory Assembly Line Analogy** > Imagine a massive biscuit factory that produces millions of biscuits daily. You, as a quality inspector, cannot weigh **every single biscuit**. Instead, you randomly pick a small handful of biscuits (a **sample**) of size $n = 30$ from the day's production and compute the average weight. The next day, you pick another random handful, and you get a *slightly different* average. > > - The **true average weight** of all biscuits ever produced is the **population mean** ($\mu$). > - Each handful's average is a **sample statistic** ($\bar{x}$). > - If you repeat this process infinitely many times, the collection of all possible sample averages forms a **distribution** — this is the **Sampling Distribution of the Mean**. ### The Three Layers of Reality $$ \underbrace{\text{True Population (unknown)}}_{\text{Parameters: } \mu, \sigma^2, p} \quad \xrightarrow{\text{repeated random sampling of size } n} \quad \underbrace{\text{Sampling Distribution of Statistic}}_{\text{Mean: } \mu_{\bar{X}}, \text{ SD: } \sigma_{\bar{X}}} \quad \xrightarrow{\text{observed once}} \quad \underbrace{\text{Single Sample Statistic}}_{\bar{x}, s^2, \hat{p} $$ > [!TIP] > **The Big Idea:** You only ever see *one* sample in real life, but the sampling distribution tells you what *would have happened* if you had drawn every possible sample. This is what lets us make **probabilistic statements** about the unknown population from a single sample. --- ## 1.3 Why Sampling Distributions Matter in Data Analytics In data analytics, we rarely have access to complete population data (think of measuring the average height of **all adult males in India**). The sampling distribution is what gives us: 1. **A measure of uncertainty** (via Standard Error). 2. **The basis for confidence intervals** (e.g., $\bar{x} \pm 1.96 \cdot SE$). 3. **The foundation for hypothesis testing** (z-tests, t-tests, chi-square tests). 4. **The justification for the Central Limit Theorem (CLT)** — the most powerful result in applied statistics. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 2 explicitly focuses on the sampling distributions of the **mean** ($\bar{X}$), the **proportion** ($\hat{p}$), and the **difference between two means/proportions**. These form the bedrock of all inferential analytics covered in later modules. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — How Sampling Distributions Arise The construction of a sampling distribution follows a precise **5-step logical procedure** (which is a guaranteed KTU exam question pattern): > **Step 1 →** Start with a population having a known (or unknown) distribution with parameters $\mu$ and $\sigma$. > > **Step 2 →** Draw a random sample of size $n$ from this population: $(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n)$. > > **Step 3 →** Compute the desired statistic (e.g., $\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$) for this sample. > > **Step 4 →** Independently repeat Steps 2 and 3 a very large number of times (theoretically, $N \to \infty$). > > **Step 5 →** Plot the probability distribution of all the observed values of the statistic. This is the **sampling distribution**. ### The "Why" Behind the Shape - The sampling distribution of $\bar{X}$ is always **less spread out** than the population distribution (because averaging smooths out extremes). - Its **center** is located at the true population mean $\mu$ (an **unbiased estimator**). - Its **shape** is approximately **Normal** by the **Central Limit Theorem**, even if the original population is skewed, provided $n$ is sufficiently large. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — it covers 80% of Part A and Part B numerical questions.** | # | Statistic | Population Parameter | Mean of Sampling Distribution | Standard Deviation (Standard Error) | Shape (for large $n$) | |---|-----------|---------------------|-------------------------------|--------------------------------------|------------------------| | 1 | Sample Mean $\bar{X}$ | $\mu, \sigma$ | $\mu_{\bar{X}} = \mu$ | $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Approximately Normal (CLT) | | 2 | Sample Mean (unknown $\sigma$, normal pop.) | $\mu$, $s$ used | $\mu_{\bar{X}} = \mu$ | $s_{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ | $t$-distribution with $n-1$ df | | 3 | Sample Proportion $\hat{p}$ | $p$ | $\mu_{\hat{p}} = p$ | $\sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{p(1-p)}{n}}$ | Approximately Normal (large $n$) | | 4 | Difference of Two Means $\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2$ | $\mu_1, \mu_2, \sigma_1, \sigma_2$ | $\mu_1 - \mu_2$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \dfrac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}$ | Approximately Normal | | 5 | Difference of Two Proportions $\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2$ | $p_1, p_2$ | $p_1 - p_2$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{p_1(1-p_1)}{n_1} + \dfrac{p_2(1-p_2)}{n_2}}$ | Approximately Normal | ### Critical Special Conditions | Condition | Sampling Distribution Form | |-----------|----------------------------| | Population is **Normal** and $\sigma$ is known | Exactly $\mathcal{N}\!\left(\mu, \dfrac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)$ for **any** $n$ | | Population is **Normal** and $\sigma$ is unknown | Exactly $t$-distribution with $df = n-1$ | | Population is **not Normal** but $n \geq 30$ | Approximately $\mathcal{N}\!\left(\mu, \dfrac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)$ (CLT) | | Sample size rule for proportions | $np \geq 5$ and $n(1-p) \geq 5$ for normal approximation | | Finite population correction (FPC) | $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{N-n}{N-1}}$ when $n/N > 0.05$ | > [!NOTE] > **In all KTU numerical problems, unless explicitly stated otherwise, assume the population is Normal, $\sigma$ is known, OR $n \geq 30$ (so CLT applies).** --- ## 2.3 Engineering & Real-World Utility | Domain | Application of Sampling Distribution | |--------|--------------------------------------| | **Quality Control (Manufacturing)** | Setting control limits on a production line: $\bar{x} \pm 3\sigma_{\bar{X}}$. | | **Medical Trials** | Estimating the true mean recovery time of a drug from a sample of patients. | | **A/B Testing (Web Analytics)** | Comparing click-through rates of two web designs using $\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2$. | | **Survey Sampling (Census/Politics)** | Computing margin of error for exit polls. | | **Machine Learning** | Bootstrap resampling and cross-validation rely on understanding sampling variability. | --- ## 2.4 Unbiasedness of Sample Mean — A Key Theorem > **Theorem (Unbiased Estimator):** The sample mean $\bar{X}$ is an unbiased estimator of the population mean $\mu$, i.e., $E[\bar{X}] = \mu$. This is why the sampling distribution of $\bar{X}$ is **centered** at $\mu$, not at some other value. This property is a guaranteed KTU Part A question. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mean and Standard Error of the Sampling Distribution of $\bar{X}$ ### Setup Let $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ be a random sample of size $n$ drawn from a population with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$. Assume all $X_i$ are **independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.)**. The sample mean is defined as: $$ \bar{X} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i $$ ### Step 1 — Derive the Mean of $\bar{X}$ $$ \begin{aligned} E[\bar{X}] &= E\!\left[ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i \right] \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} E[X_i] \quad \text{(linearity of expectation)} \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mu \quad \text{(since each } X_i \text{ has mean } \mu\text{)} \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{n} \cdot n\mu \\[6pt] &= \mu \end{aligned} $$ ✅ **Result:** The sampling distribution of $\bar{X}$ is centered at $\mu$. ### Step 2 — Derive the Variance of $\bar{X}$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Var}(\bar{X}) &= \text{Var}\!\left( \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i \right) \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{n^2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Var}(X_i) \quad \text{(independence } \Rightarrow \text{ variances add)} \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{n^2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma^2 \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{n^2} \cdot n\sigma^2 \\[6pt] &= \frac{\sigma^2}{n} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Standard Error $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_{\bar{X}} &= \sqrt{\text{Var}(\bar{X})} \\[6pt] &= \sqrt{\frac{\sigma^2}{n}} \\[6pt] &= \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Increasing the sample size by a factor of $k$ reduces the standard error by a factor of $\sqrt{k}$. To **halve** the SE, you must **quadruple** the sample size. This is a common KTU 14-mark question. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sampling Distribution of Sample Proportion $\hat{p}$ ### Setup Let $X$ = number of "successes" in a sample of size $n$, where each observation is a Bernoulli trial with success probability $p$. Then $X \sim \text{Binomial}(n, p)$, and $$ \hat{p} = \frac{X}{n} $$ ### Step 1 — Mean of $\hat{p}$ $$ \begin{aligned} E[\hat{p}] &= E\!\left[ \frac{X}{n} \right] = \frac{E[X]}{n} = \frac{np}{n} = p \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Variance of $\hat{p}$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Var}(\hat{p}) &= \frac{\text{Var}(X)}{n^2} = \frac{np(1-p)}{n^2} = \frac{p(1-p)}{n} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Standard Error $$ \sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Large Sample Normal Approximation (KTU Favourite):** When $np \geq 5$ and $n(1-p) \geq 5$, $$ \hat{p} \;\dot\sim\; \mathcal{N}\!\left( p, \; \frac{p(1-p)}{n} \right) $$ This is used to compute probabilities such as $P(\hat{p} < c)$ or for constructing confidence intervals. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Style) > **Problem:** A packaging machine fills bottles with a mean volume of $\mu = 500$ mL and standard deviation $\sigma = 20$ mL. A random sample of $n = 64$ bottles is taken. Find: > (a) The mean and standard error of the sampling distribution of $\bar{X}$. > (b) The probability that the sample mean is between 495 mL and 505 mL. ### Solution **(a) Mean and Standard Error:** $$ \mu_{\bar{X}} = \mu = 500 \text{ mL} $$ $$ \sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{20}{\sqrt{64}} = \frac{20}{8} = 2.5 \text{ mL} $$ **(b) Probability Calculation** (Using CLT, $\bar{X} \sim \mathcal{N}(500, 2.5^2)$): $$ \begin{aligned} P(495 \le \bar{X} \le 505) &= P\!\left( \frac{495 - 500}{2.5} \le Z \le \frac{505 - 500}{2.5} \right) \\[6pt] &= P(-2 \le Z \le 2) \\[6pt] &= \Phi(2) - \Phi(-2) \\[6pt] &= 0.9772 - 0.0228 \\[6pt] &= 0.9544 \end{aligned} $$ ✅ **Answer:** $\mu_{\bar{X}} = 500$ mL, $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = 2.5$ mL, and $P(495 \le \bar{X} \le 505) = 0.9544$. --- ## 3.4 Difference Between Two Sample Means — The Bivariate Case For two independent samples $(n_1, \bar{X}_1, \sigma_1)$ and $(n_2, \bar{X}_2, \sigma_2)$: $$ \mu_{\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2} = \mu_1 - \mu_2 $$ $$ \sigma_{\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2} = \sqrt{ \frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2} } $$ For large $n_1, n_2$ (or both populations Normal): $$ \bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2 \;\sim\; \mathcal{N}\!\left( \mu_1 - \mu_2, \; \frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2} \right) $$ This is the cornerstone of the **two-sample z-test** (covered in the next module). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Population → Sample → Sampling Distribution (Conceptual Map) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[POPULATION
Mean mu, Variance sigma squared] --> B[Step 1: Draw Random Sample of size n] B --> C[Step 2: Compute Statistic e.g. X-bar] C --> D[Step 3: Repeat infinitely many times] D --> E[Step 4: Collect all sample statistics] E --> F[SAMPLING DISTRIBUTION
Mean mu, SE sigma over root n] F --> G[Shape: Normal by CLT for large n] G --> H[Used for Confidence Intervals
and Hypothesis Testing] style A fill:#f9d5e5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#d5e8f9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style H fill:#d5f9d5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## 4.2 Comparison: Population Distribution vs. Sampling Distribution ```mermaid graph LR P[Population Distribution
Mean mu, SD sigma
Shape: ANY] -- "Draw n samples
(theoretically infinite)" --> S[Sampling Distribution of X-bar
Mean mu, SD sigma/root n
Shape: APPROXIMATELY NORMAL] S --> Q1[Wider spread
if n is small] S --> Q2[Narrower spread
if n is large] style P fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style S fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style Q1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style Q2 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` --- ## 4.3 Central Limit Theorem — Visual Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Original Population
Any Shape] --> B{Is n large enough?} B -- "Yes, n >= 30" --> C[Sampling Distribution
Approximately NORMAL
Mean = mu
SD = sigma / root n] B -- "No, n small
and pop. not normal" --> D[Sampling Distribution
Shape UNKNOWN
Do not assume normal] B -- "Pop. already NORMAL
and sigma known" --> E[Sampling Distribution
EXACTLY NORMAL
for ANY n] style A fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## 4.4 Effect of Sample Size on Standard Error ```mermaid graph TD N[Increase Sample Size n] --> SE1[SE = sigma / root n DECREASES] SE1 --> CI[Narrower Confidence Interval] SE1 --> P[Margin of Error SHRINKS] SE1 --> A[More Precise Estimate of mu] style N fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style SE1 fill:#d5e8f9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style A fill:#d5f9d5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## 4.5 Functional Block: Sampling Distribution of Sample Proportion ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Population Proportion p unknown] --> S1[Draw n independent Bernoulli trials] S1 --> X[X = number of successes
X ~ Binomial n, p] X --> PH[Compute p-hat = X / n] PH --> M[Mean of p-hat = p] PH --> V[Variance of p-hat = p(1-p)/n] PH --> N{Is np and n 1-p both at least 5?} N -- "Yes" --> NORM[p-hat ~ Approximately NORMAL p, p1-p/n] N -- "No" --> EXACT[Use Exact Binomial Distribution] style P1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style NORM fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style EXACT fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define the term "Sampling Distribution." How does it differ from a sample distribution?** **Model Answer:** A **Sampling Distribution** is the probability distribution of a sample statistic (e.g., $\bar{X}$, $\hat{p}$) obtained from all possible samples of a fixed size $n$ drawn from the same population. | Sample Distribution | Sampling Distribution | |---------------------|----------------------| | Refers to the spread of individual observations **within a single sample**. | Refers to the spread of a **statistic** (e.g., $\bar{x}$) across **many samples** of the same size $n$. | | Empirical, descriptive. | Theoretical, probabilistic. | | One distribution per sample. | One distribution per statistic per sample size. | *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for sample dist. + 1 for contrast table)* --- ### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **State the Central Limit Theorem. Why is it important in data analytics?** **Model Answer:** > **Central Limit Theorem (CLT):** Given a population with mean $\mu$ and finite variance $\sigma^2$, the sampling distribution of the sample mean $\bar{X}$ approaches a **Normal distribution** with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2/n$ as the sample size $n$ becomes large (typically $n \geq 30$). **Importance:** 1. Enables **inferential statistics** (confidence intervals, hypothesis tests) even when the population is non-normal. 2. Allows use of $Z$-tables and $t$-tables for probability calculations. 3. Forms the theoretical foundation for **regression, A/B testing, and machine learning bootstrapping**. *(3 marks: 1.5 for statement + 1.5 for importance)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the mean and standard error of the sampling distribution of the sample mean $\bar{X}$. State any assumptions used. **(b) [7 Marks]** A random sample of size $n = 36$ is drawn from a population with mean $\mu = 80$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 12$. Find the probability that the sample mean lies between 78 and 83. --- ### **Model Solution for Question A** #### **Part (a) — Derivation** Let $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ be a random sample from a population with $E[X_i] = \mu$ and $\text{Var}(X_i) = \sigma^2$, with all $X_i$ **independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.)**. **Assumption 1:** $E[X_i] = \mu$ for all $i$ (finite mean). **Assumption 2:** $\text{Var}(X_i) = \sigma^2$ (finite variance). **Assumption 3:** $X_i$'s are mutually independent. **Mean of $\bar{X}$:** $$ E[\bar{X}] = E\!\left[\frac{1}{n}\sum X_i\right] = \frac{1}{n}\sum E[X_i] = \frac{1}{n} \cdot n\mu = \mu $$ **Variance of $\bar{X}$:** $$ \text{Var}(\bar{X}) = \frac{1}{n^2}\sum \text{Var}(X_i) = \frac{1}{n^2} \cdot n\sigma^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{n} $$ **Standard Error:** $$ \sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} $$ **Shape:** By CLT, for large $n$, $\bar{X} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mu, \sigma^2/n\right)$. *(7 marks: 2 for setup/assumptions + 2 for mean + 2 for variance + 1 for SE/shape)* --- #### **Part (b) — Numerical** Given: $n = 36$, $\mu = 80$, $\sigma = 12$. $$ \sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{12}{\sqrt{36}} = \frac{12}{6} = 2 $$ Standardize: $$ P(78 \le \bar{X} \le 83) = P\!\left( \frac{78 - 80}{2} \le Z \le \frac{83 - 80}{2} \right) = P(-1 \le Z \le 1.5) $$ $$ = \Phi(1.5) - \Phi(-1) = 0.9332 - 0.1587 = 0.7745 $$ ✅ **Answer:** Probability $= 0.7745$ (i.e., 77.45%). *(7 marks: 2 for SE + 3 for Z-transform + 2 for final answer)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the sampling distribution of the sample proportion $\hat{p}$. Derive its mean and standard error. **(b) [7 Marks]** In a manufacturing process, the defect rate is historically $p = 0.05$. A random sample of 200 items is inspected. Find the probability that the sample proportion of defects exceeds 0.08. --- ### **Model Solution for Question B** #### **Part (a) — Theoretical Explanation** Consider a population where each element is a **Bernoulli trial**: success with probability $p$, failure with probability $1-p$. In a sample of size $n$, let $X$ = number of successes. Then $X \sim \text{Binomial}(n, p)$. The sample proportion is: $$ \hat{p} = \frac{X}{n} $$ **Mean of $\hat{p}$:** $$ E[\hat{p}] = \frac{E[X]}{n} = \frac{np}{n} = p $$ **Variance of $\hat{p}$:** $$ \text{Var}(\hat{p}) = \frac{\text{Var}(X)}{n^2} = \frac{np(1-p)}{n^2} = \frac{p(1-p)}{n} $$ **Standard Error:** $$ \sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}} $$ **Normal Approximation (CLT for proportions):** If $np \geq 5$ and $n(1-p) \geq 5$, then $$ \hat{p} \;\dot\sim\; \mathcal{N}\!\left(p, \frac{p(1-p)}{n}\right) $$ *(7 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for mean + 2 for variance + 1 for SE + 1 for normal approximation condition)* --- #### **Part (b) — Numerical** Given: $p = 0.05$, $n = 200$, find $P(\hat{p} > 0.08)$. **Check conditions:** $np = 200 \times 0.05 = 10 \geq 5$ ✓ and $n(1-p) = 190 \geq 5$ ✓. Normal approximation valid. **Standard Error:** $$ \sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.05 \times 0.95}{200}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.0475}{200}} = \sqrt{0.0002375} \approx 0.01541 $$ **Z-score for $\hat{p} = 0.08$:** $$ Z = \frac{0.08 - 0.05}{0.01541} = \frac{0.03}{0.01541} \approx 1.947 $$ **Probability:** $$ P(\hat{p} > 0.08) = P(Z > 1.947) = 1 - \Phi(1.947) \approx 1 - 0.9742 = 0.0258 $$ ✅ **Answer:** Probability $\approx 0.0258$ (i.e., about 2.58%). *(7 marks: 1 for condition check + 2 for SE + 2 for Z + 2 for final probability)* --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting to use $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$ instead of $\sigma$** — the most frequent error. The standard deviation *of the sample mean* is $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \sigma/\sqrt{n}$, not $\sigma$. > > 2. **Not stating CLT assumptions:** When applying the Normal approximation, you MUST verify that $n \geq 30$ OR that the population is Normal. > > 3. **Forgetting the FPC:** If $n/N > 0.05$ (i.e., sampling more than 5% of the population), you must multiply the standard error by $\sqrt{(N-n)/(N-1)}$. > > 4. **Mixing up $p$ and $\hat{p}$:** In formula sheets, $p$ is the population parameter (unknown in practice), but in KTU numericals it is usually given. Do NOT replace it with $\hat{p}$ when computing the SE. > > 5. **Skipping the "Why" in 14-mark answers:** KTU examiners award at least **1–2 marks** for stating the underlying theorem or property (e.g., "by CLT, $\bar{X}$ is approximately Normal" or "by linearity of expectation"). > > 6. **No standard Normal table reference:** Always write "From standard Normal tables, $\Phi(1.5) = 0.9332$" to show complete working. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Introduction to Sampling Distributions** ### ✅ Core Concepts - [x] **Sampling Distribution** = distribution of a statistic over all possible samples of size $n$. - [x] **Statistic** is to a **Sample** as a **Parameter** is to a **Population**. - [x] **Sample Mean** $\bar{X}$ is an **unbiased estimator** of $\mu$, i.e., $E[\bar{X}] = \mu$. - [x] **Sample Proportion** $\hat{p} = X/n$ is an **unbiased estimator** of $p$. ### ✅ Key Formulas (Memorize!) - [x] $\mu_{\bar{X}} = \mu$ - [x] $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \sigma/\sqrt{n}$ (or $s/\sqrt{n}$ when $\sigma$ unknown) - [x] $\mu_{\hat{p}} = p$ - [x] $\sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{p(1-p)/n}$ - [x] $\sigma_{\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2/n_1 + \sigma_2^2/n_2}$ ### ✅ Central Limit Theorem - [x] **For means:** $\bar{X} \;\dot\sim\; \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2/n)$ when $n \geq 30$ OR population is Normal. - [x] **For proportions:** $\hat{p} \;\dot\sim\; \mathcal{N}(p, p(1-p)/n)$ when $np \geq 5$ and $n(1-p) \geq 5$. ### ✅ Conditions & Special Cases - [x] Use **FPC** $\sqrt{(N-n)/(N-1)}$ when $n/N > 0.05$. - [x] Use **$t$-distribution** with $df = n-1$ when population is Normal and $\sigma$ is unknown (introduced in next module). - [x] Standard error is the **square root** of the variance of the sampling distribution. ### ✅ Examiner Triggers - [x] Always show **Z-transformation** steps in probability problems. - [x] Always state **CLT** as justification for Normal approximation. - [x] Always state **assumptions** (i.i.d., finite variance) in derivation questions. ### ✅ Common Question Patterns - "Derive the mean and standard error of the sampling distribution of $\bar{X}$." → **7 marks derivation** - "Find $P(a < \bar{X} < b)$ given $\mu, \sigma, n$." → **7 marks numerical** - "Explain sampling distribution of $\hat{p}$" → **7 marks theory** - "Find $P(\hat{p} > c)$ given $p, n$." → **7 marks numerical** --- > **END OF NOTES — MODULE 2: INTRODUCTION TO SAMPLING DISTRIBUTIONS**

topics.title: Central Limit Theorem, Estimators: Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET501 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Central Limit Theorem, Estimators: Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Central Limit Theorem (CLT)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> The **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)** is a foundational theorem in probability and statistics which states that the **arithmetic mean of a sufficiently large number of independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) random variables** — *each with finite mean and finite non-zero variance* — will be **approximately normally distributed**, *regardless of the underlying probability distribution of the original variables*.

**Mathematical Statement (Lindberg–Lévy form):**
Let $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ be a sequence of **i.i.d. random variables** with:
* Mean: $E[X_i] = \mu$ (finite)
* Variance: $Var(X_i) = \sigma^2 < \infty$ (finite and non-zero)

Then, as $n \to \infty$, the standardized sample mean converges **in distribution** to the standard normal:

$$\frac{\bar{X}_n - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$$

where $\bar{X}_n = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$.

---

### 🔍 Intuitive Analogy — "The Rice Bag Factory"

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine a Kerala rice packaging factory where individual rice bags are filled by a noisy automated machine. The weight of a **single** bag could follow *any* weird distribution — maybe slightly skewed, maybe multi-modal, maybe uniform (between, say, **24.8 kg** and **25.4 kg**). It is unpredictable for a single bag.

Now, suppose a quality inspector **samples 50 bags at a time** and computes the **average weight of each sample**. If the inspector plots the histogram of *hundreds of such sample averages*, the result will be a beautiful, symmetric, **bell-shaped (Gaussian) curve** — even though the *individual* bag weights were NOT normally distributed.

**The "magic" is the CLT:** averages of large samples tame the chaos of the underlying distribution and produce normalcy. This is *why* the normal distribution is ubiquitous in Data Analytics.

| Real-World Mapping | Mathematical Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Individual rice bag weight | Random variable $X_i$ (any distribution) |
| Sample of $n = 50$ bags | Sample $\{X_1, \ldots, X_n\}$ |
| Mean of 50 bags | Sample mean $\bar{X}_n$ |
| Histogram of 1000 sample means | Sampling distribution of $\bar{X}_n$ |
| Bell shape that emerges | $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2/n)$ |

---

## 1.2 Estimators — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> An **estimator** is a *rule* (often expressed as a function) that tells us how to calculate an **estimate** of a population parameter based on the observed sample data.
> * If the output is a **single value**, it is a **point estimator** (e.g., $\bar{X}$ for $\mu$).
> * If the output is a **range** with a confidence level, it is an **interval estimator** (e.g., a 95% confidence interval for $\mu$).

**Notation:** A population parameter is denoted $\theta$. Its estimator from a sample is $\hat{\theta}_n$ (a *random variable*, since the sample is random).

**Examples:**
* $\bar{X}_n = \frac{1}{n}\sum X_i$ is an estimator of the population mean $\mu$.
* $S_n^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum (X_i - \bar{X}_n)^2$ is an estimator of the population variance $\sigma^2$.
* The sample proportion $\hat{p}$ is an estimator of the population proportion $p$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Central Limit Theorem — Conditions and Mechanics

The CLT is **not** a single theorem but a family of related results. The Lindberg–Lévy CLT (most common in KTU) requires:

* **Independence:** The random variables $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ must be mutually independent.
* **Identical Distribution:** All $X_i$ must be drawn from the same parent distribution.
* **Finite Mean & Variance:** $E[X_i] = \mu$ and $Var(X_i) = \sigma^2$ must both exist and be finite.
* **Large Sample Size:** The rule-of-thumb is $n \geq 30$ for most non-extreme distributions. For *highly skewed* populations, $n \geq 50$ is preferred.

### 2.1.1 The Three Equivalent Forms of the CLT

| Form | Statement | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Sum Form** | $\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i - n\mu}{\sigma\sqrt{n}} \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$ | When summing independent observations |
| **Mean Form** | $\frac{\bar{X}_n - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$ | Most common in inference; basis of confidence intervals |
| **Proportion Form** | $\frac{\hat{p} - p}{\sqrt{p(1-p)/n}} \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$ | Binary / categorical outcomes (Bernoulli trials) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the CLT is the "Holy Grail" of Data Analytics:**
> 1. It justifies the use of the **normal distribution** for inference even when the population is non-normal.
> 2. It enables **hypothesis testing** (Z-tests, t-tests) and **confidence intervals**.
> 3. It underlies **regression**, **ANOVA**, and almost every parametric test in the KTU syllabus.
> 4. In modern **ML**, it underpins **bootstrap resampling** theory and the **Central Limit Theorem for Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)** in deep learning.

---

## 2.2 Properties of Estimators

> [!NOTE]
> An estimator $\hat{\theta}$ is "good" or "bad" depending on whether it satisfies certain mathematical properties. The five canonical properties in the **KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus** are: **Unbiasedness, Consistency, Efficiency, Sufficiency, and Mean Square Error (MSE).**

### 2.2.1 Unbiasedness

* **Definition:** An estimator $\hat{\theta}$ is **unbiased** for $\theta$ if its expected value equals the true parameter:

$$E[\hat{\theta}] = \theta \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad Bias(\hat{\theta}) = E[\hat{\theta} - \theta] = 0$$

* **Example:** $E[\bar{X}_n] = \mu$ (sample mean is unbiased for population mean). $E[S_n^2] = \sigma^2$ (sample variance with denominator $n-1$ is unbiased).

### 2.2.2 Consistency

* **Definition:** An estimator $\hat{\theta}_n$ is **consistent** if it converges *in probability* to the true parameter as the sample size grows without bound:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} P\!\left(\vert \hat{\theta}_n - \theta \vert < \epsilon\right) = 1 \quad \text{for every } \epsilon > 0$$

* **Mnemonic:** *"Big data = Good estimate"*. Consistency is a *large-sample* property; unbiasedness is a *finite-sample* property.

### 2.2.3 Efficiency

* **Definition:** Among all unbiased estimators of $\theta$, the one with the **smallest variance** is the **most efficient** (or **minimum-variance unbiased estimator, MVUE**).
* **Relative Efficiency** of $\hat{\theta}_1$ with respect to $\hat{\theta}_2$:

$$\text{eff}(\hat{\theta}_1, \hat{\theta}_2) = \frac{Var(\hat{\theta}_2)}{Var(\hat{\theta}_1)}$$

If this ratio $> 1$, then $\hat{\theta}_1$ is more efficient than $\hat{\theta}_2$.

### 2.2.4 Sufficiency

* **Definition:** A statistic $T(X)$ is **sufficient** for $\theta$ if it *encapsulates all the information* in the sample about $\theta$. Knowing $T$ makes the raw data $X$ redundant for inference on $\theta$.
* **Formal:** $f(X_1, \ldots, X_n \mid T, \theta) = f(X_1, \ldots, X_n \mid T)$ (no $\theta$ in the conditional density of data given $T$).
* **Example:** $\bar{X}_n$ is sufficient for $\mu$ when the population variance $\sigma^2$ is known and the population is normal.

### 2.2.5 Mean Square Error (MSE)

* **Definition:** A *loss-function based* criterion combining bias and variance into a single number:

$$MSE(\hat{\theta}) = E\!\left[(\hat{\theta} - \theta)^2\right] = Var(\hat{\theta}) + \big(Bias(\hat{\theta})\big)^2$$

* **Interpretation:** A small MSE is good. An estimator can have a *small bias but large variance*, and still be worse than a slightly biased estimator with *tiny variance*.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CLT — Sum Form | $Z = \dfrac{\sum X_i - n\mu}{\sigma \sqrt{n}} \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$ | i.i.d., $n \geq 30$, finite $\sigma^2$ |
| 2 | CLT — Mean Form | $Z = \dfrac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$ | i.i.d., $n \geq 30$ |
| 3 | CLT — Proportion Form | $Z = \dfrac{\hat{p} - p}{\sqrt{p(1-p)/n}} \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$ | $np \geq 5$ and $n(1-p) \geq 5$ |
| 4 | Unbiasedness | $E[\hat{\theta}] = \theta$ | Finite expectation |
| 5 | Bias | $Bias(\hat{\theta}) = E[\hat{\theta}] - \theta$ | Scalar |
| 6 | Consistency | $\forall \epsilon > 0, \; \lim_{n \to \infty} P(\vert \hat{\theta}_n - \theta \vert < \epsilon) = 1$ | $n \to \infty$ |
| 7 | Relative Efficiency | $\text{eff}(\hat{\theta}_1, \hat{\theta}_2) = \dfrac{Var(\hat{\theta}_2)}{Var(\hat{\theta}_1)}$ | Both unbiased |
| 8 | Cramér–Rao Lower Bound | $Var(\hat{\theta}) \geq \dfrac{1}{n \cdot \mathcal{I}(\theta)}$ | Where $\mathcal{I}$ is Fisher Information |
| 9 | MSE Decomposition | $MSE(\hat{\theta}) = Var(\hat{\theta}) + Bias^2(\hat{\theta})$ | Always valid |
| 10 | Variance of Sample Mean | $Var(\bar{X}) = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{n}$ | i.i.d. |
| 11 | Asymptotic Normality | $\sqrt{n}(\hat{\theta}_n - \theta) \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2_\theta)$ | $\hat{\theta}_n$ is a regular estimator |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of CLT / Estimator Properties |
|---|---|
| **Telecommunications** | Estimating average packet delay from a finite sample of network logs. |
| **Quality Engineering** | X-bar control charts rely on the CLT to set 3$\sigma$ control limits for monitoring factory processes. |
| **Machine Learning** | The MSE criterion is the optimization target in linear regression ($\min \sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$). |
| **A/B Testing (Web Analytics)** | Click-through rates are estimated using $\hat{p}$ with CLT-based confidence intervals to detect statistically significant differences. |
| **Financial Analytics** | Estimating Value-at-Risk (VaR) of a portfolio using sample means and sample variances from historical returns. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Sample Mean is Unbiased for Population Mean

**Claim:** $E[\bar{X}_n] = \mu$.

**Setup:** Let $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ be i.i.d. with $E[X_i] = \mu$ and $Var(X_i) = \sigma^2$.

**Step 1 — Write the estimator explicitly.**

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i = \frac{1}{n}\,X_1 + \frac{1}{n}\,X_2 + \cdots + \frac{1}{n}\,X_n$$

**Step 2 — Apply the linearity of expectation to each term.**

$$E[\bar{X}_n] = E\!\left[\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right]$$

**Step 3 — Pull the constant $1/n$ outside the expectation operator (linearity).**

$$E[\bar{X}_n] = \frac{1}{n}\, E\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right]$$

**Step 4 — Apply linearity of expectation across the sum.**

$$E[\bar{X}_n] = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} E[X_i]$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $E[X_i] = \mu$ for all $i$ (identical distribution).**

$$E[\bar{X}_n] = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mu = \frac{1}{n} \cdot n \cdot \mu$$

**Step 6 — Simplify to reach the conclusion.**

$$E[\bar{X}_n] = \mu \quad \blacksquare$$

Hence the bias is $Bias(\bar{X}_n) = E[\bar{X}_n] - \mu = 0$, and $\bar{X}_n$ is an **unbiased** estimator of $\mu$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Variance of the Sample Mean

**Claim:** $Var(\bar{X}_n) = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{n}$.

**Step 1 — Begin with the same definition.**

$$Var(\bar{X}_n) = Var\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right)$$

**Step 2 — Pull out the constant $1/n$ (variance of $cX = c^2 \cdot Var(X)$).**

$$Var(\bar{X}_n) = \frac{1}{n^2}\, Var\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right)$$

**Step 3 — Expand the variance of the sum (using independence of the $X_i$).**

$$Var\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Var(X_i) + 2\!\!\sum_{i<j}\!\! Cov(X_i, X_j)$$

**Step 4 — Independence forces $Cov(X_i, X_j) = 0$ for all $i \neq j$.**

$$Var\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Var(X_i)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $Var(X_i) = \sigma^2$ for all $i$ (identical distribution).**

$$Var\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma^2 = n\sigma^2$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back into the expression for $Var(\bar{X}_n)$.**

$$Var(\bar{X}_n) = \frac{1}{n^2} \cdot n\sigma^2$$

**Step 7 — Final simplification.**

$$Var(\bar{X}_n) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Error (SE):** The square root of this variance, $\sigma / \sqrt{n}$, is the **standard error of the mean** — the most quoted quantity in the KTU CLT module.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: MSE Decomposition

**Claim:** $MSE(\hat{\theta}) = Var(\hat{\theta}) + \big(Bias(\hat{\theta})\big)^2$.

**Step 1 — Begin with the definition of MSE.**

$$MSE(\hat{\theta}) = E\!\left[(\hat{\theta} - \theta)^2\right]$$

**Step 2 — Add and subtract $E[\hat{\theta}]$ inside the squared term (algebraic trick).**

$$MSE(\hat{\theta}) = E\!\left[\big((\hat{\theta} - E[\hat{\theta}]) + (E[\hat{\theta}] - \theta)\big)^2\right]$$

**Step 3 — Let $A = \hat{\theta} - E[\hat{\theta}]$ and $B = E[\hat{\theta}] - \theta = Bias(\hat{\theta})$ and expand the square.**

$$MSE = E\!\left[A^2 + 2AB + B^2\right]$$

**Step 4 — Distribute the expectation operator across the three terms.**

$$MSE = E[A^2] + 2B \cdot E[A] + B^2$$

**Step 5 — Note that $E[A] = E[\hat{\theta} - E[\hat{\theta}]] = E[\hat{\theta}] - E[\hat{\theta}] = 0$.**

$$MSE = E[A^2] + 2B \cdot 0 + B^2$$

**Step 6 — Recognize $E[A^2] = Var(\hat{\theta})$ by definition.**

$$MSE = Var(\hat{\theta}) + B^2 = Var(\hat{\theta}) + \big(Bias(\hat{\theta})\big)^2 \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This decomposition is why *regularization* in machine learning (e.g., Ridge regression) is effective — it explicitly trades a small amount of **bias** for a *large reduction* in **variance**, leading to a *smaller* overall MSE on unseen data.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Consistency of the Sample Mean (Heuristic)

**Claim:** $\bar{X}_n \xrightarrow{P} \mu$ as $n \to \infty$ (i.e., $\bar{X}_n$ is consistent for $\mu$).

**Step 1 — Use the result of the previous section.**

$$Var(\bar{X}_n) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Chebyshev's inequality: for any $\epsilon > 0$,**

$$P\!\left(\vert \bar{X}_n - \mu \vert \geq \epsilon\right) \leq \frac{Var(\bar{X}_n)}{\epsilon^2} = \frac{\sigma^2}{n\epsilon^2}$$

**Step 3 — Take the limit as $n \to \infty$.**

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} P\!\left(\vert \bar{X}_n - \mu \vert \geq \epsilon\right) \leq \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\sigma^2}{n\epsilon^2} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Equivalently, the probability of being *within* $\epsilon$ of $\mu$ tends to 1.**

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} P\!\left(\vert \bar{X}_n - \mu \vert < \epsilon\right) = 1 \quad \blacksquare$$

Hence $\bar{X}_n$ is a **consistent** estimator of $\mu$.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem (Dec 2023 style):** The lifetimes (in hours) of electric bulbs produced by a factory have a mean $\mu = 800$ hrs and standard deviation $\sigma = 100$ hrs. A random sample of $n = 64$ bulbs is taken. Find the probability that the sample mean lifetime is between **790 hrs and 810 hrs**.

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**

$$n = 64, \quad \mu = 800, \quad \sigma = 100$$

**Step 2 — Standard error of the mean.**

$$SE = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{100}{\sqrt{64}} = \frac{100}{8} = 12.5$$

**Step 3 — Apply the CLT. By the mean form of the CLT,**

$$Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} = \frac{\bar{X} - 800}{12.5} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$$

**Step 4 — Standardize the bounds.**

$$Z_1 = \frac{790 - 800}{12.5} = \frac{-10}{12.5} = -0.80$$

$$Z_2 = \frac{810 - 800}{12.5} = \frac{10}{12.5} = 0.80$$

**Step 5 — Compute the probability using the standard normal table.**

$$P(790 < \bar{X} < 810) = P(-0.80 < Z < 0.80)$$

$$= \Phi(0.80) - \Phi(-0.80) = 2\,\Phi(0.80) - 1$$

Using the standard normal table, $\Phi(0.80) = 0.7881$.

$$P = 2(0.7881) - 1 = 1.5762 - 1 = 0.5762$$

**Step 6 — Final answer.**

$$P(790 < \bar{X} < 810) \approx 0.5762 \quad \text{or} \quad 57.62\%$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: CLT Pipeline

The diagram below illustrates the **functional architecture** of how the Central Limit Theorem is invoked inside a typical Data Analytics workflow — from raw data to inference.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[POPULATION X with arbitrary distribution fX] --> B[DRAW INDEPENDENT SAMPLE X1, X2, ..., Xn]
    B --> C[COMPUTE SAMPLE STATISTIC e.g. sample mean Xbar]
    C --> D{IS n SUFFICIENTLY LARGE?}
    D -- NO n less than 30 --> E[USE EXACT DISTRIBUTION e.g. t, chi-square]
    D -- YES n greater or equal 30 --> F[APPLY CENTRAL LIMIT THEOREM]
    F --> G[STANDARDIZE Xbar to Z = Xbar minus mu divided by sigma over root n]
    G --> H[Z APPROXIMATELY FOLLOWS STANDARD NORMAL N0,1]
    H --> I[INFERENCE TASKS]
    I --> I1[CONFIDENCE INTERVALS]
    I --> I2[Z TESTS AND HYPOTHESIS TESTS]
    I --> I3[CONTROL CHARTS IN QUALITY ENGINEERING]
    I --> I4[BOOTSTRAP RESAMPLING IN ML]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style B fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style C fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style D fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style E fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style F fill:#70ad47,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style G fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#70ad47,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style I fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style I1 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style I2 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style I3 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style I4 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Estimator Property Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AN ESTIMATOR THETA HAT IS PROPOSED] --> B{COMPUTE EXPECTED VALUE}
    B -- E[THETA HAT] EQUALS THETA --> C[UNBIASED]
    B -- E[THETA HAT] NOT EQUALS THETA --> D[BIASED]
    C --> E{CHECK VARIANCE AS n GROWS}
    D --> E
    E -- VARIANCE TENDS TO ZERO --> F[CONSISTENT]
    E -- VARIANCE DOES NOT TEND TO ZERO --> G[NOT CONSISTENT]
    F --> H{COMPARE VARIANCE WITH OTHER UNBIASED ESTIMATORS}
    H -- SMALLEST VARIANCE --> I[MVUE MOST EFFICIENT]
    H -- LARGER VARIANCE --> J[LESS EFFICIENT]
    F --> K{CHECK FISHER NEYMAN FACTORIZATION}
    K -- fX G FACTORS AS gT TIMES hX --> L[SUFFICIENT]
    K -- DOES NOT FACTOR --> M[NOT SUFFICIENT]
    C --> N[COMPUTE MSE = VARIANCE PLUS BIAS SQUARED]
    D --> N
    N --> O[MSE IS A SINGLE SCALAR SUMMARY OF ESTIMATOR QUALITY]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style C fill:#70ad47,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style D fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style F fill:#70ad47,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style G fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style I fill:#70ad47,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style J fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style L fill:#70ad47,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style M fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style N fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style O fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Relationship Between Estimator Properties (Concept Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[UNBIASEDNESS] --> P5[MSE MINIMIZATION]
    P2[CONSISTENCY] --> P5
    P3[EFFICIENCY MVUE] --> P5
    P4[SUFFICIENCY] --> P3
    P1 --> P2
    P3 --> P2
    P4 --> P5

    P1 --- P2
    P2 --- P3
    P3 --- P4

    style P1 fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style P2 fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style P3 fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style P4 fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style P5 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Unbiasedness + Low Variance ⇒ Efficiency ⇒ Consistency (in most regular cases). Sufficiency is a *structural* property of the data-generating process, while MSE is the *operational* loss function used to *rank* estimators in practice.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]**
**State the Central Limit Theorem. What are the essential conditions for its application?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)** states that if $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ are **independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.)** random variables with **finite mean** $\mu$ and **finite variance** $\sigma^2$, then the standardized sample mean

$$Z_n = \frac{\bar{X}_n - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0, 1) \quad \text{as } n \to \infty$$

> **Essential Conditions:**
> 1. **Independence** of the $X_i$'s.
> 2. **Identical distribution** of the $X_i$'s.
> 3. **Finite mean** $E[X_i] = \mu$ and **finite non-zero variance** $Var(X_i) = \sigma^2$.
> 4. **Sample size $n$ is sufficiently large** (rule of thumb: $n \geq 30$; for highly skewed populations, $n \geq 50$).

> The CLT holds **regardless of the shape of the parent population distribution**, which makes it the cornerstone of inferential statistics.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define an unbiased estimator. Show that the sample mean is an unbiased estimator of the population mean.**

**Model Answer:**

> An estimator $\hat{\theta}$ of a population parameter $\theta$ is called **unbiased** if $E[\hat{\theta}] = \theta$.

> **Proof that $\bar{X}_n$ is unbiased for $\mu$:**
> Let $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ be i.i.d. random variables with mean $\mu$. The sample mean is $\bar{X}_n = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$. Taking expectation:

$$E[\bar{X}_n] = E\!\left[\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right] = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} E[X_i] = \frac{1}{n} \cdot n\mu = \mu$$

> Hence $E[\bar{X}_n] = \mu$, so the sample mean is an **unbiased estimator** of the population mean.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Format

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — *On Central Limit Theorem*

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A manufacturing process produces steel rods whose lengths follow a uniform distribution on the interval $[98, 102]$ cm. A random sample of $n = 36$ rods is taken. Using the Central Limit Theorem, find the probability that the **sample mean length** lies between **99.5 cm and 100.5 cm**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A call center receives, on average, $\lambda = 4$ calls per minute. For a random sample of $n = 50$ one-minute intervals, use the CLT to approximate the probability that the **total number of calls** received exceeds **220**.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q. A (a)

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters of the uniform distribution on $[98, 102]$.**

$$\mu = \frac{a + b}{2} = \frac{98 + 102}{2} = 100 \text{ cm}$$

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{(b - a)^2}{12} = \frac{(102 - 98)^2}{12} = \frac{16}{12} = \frac{4}{3}$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{4}{3}} \approx 1.1547 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the CLT. By the mean form of the CLT,**

$$Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the standard error.**

$$SE = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{1.1547}{\sqrt{36}} = \frac{1.1547}{6} \approx 0.1925$$

**Step 4 — Standardize the bounds.**

$$Z_1 = \frac{99.5 - 100}{0.1925} = \frac{-0.5}{0.1925} \approx -2.598$$

$$Z_2 = \frac{100.5 - 100}{0.1925} = \frac{0.5}{0.1925} \approx +2.598$$

**Step 5 — Compute the probability using the standard normal table.**

$$P(99.5 < \bar{X} < 100.5) = P(-2.598 < Z < 2.598)$$

$$= 2\,\Phi(2.598) - 1$$

From standard normal tables, $\Phi(2.60) \approx 0.9953$, so $\Phi(2.598) \approx 0.9953$.

$$P \approx 2(0.9953) - 1 = 1.9906 - 1 = 0.9906$$

**Final Answer:** $P(99.5 < \bar{X} < 100.5) \approx 0.9906$ (i.e., about **99.06%**).

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q. A (b)

**Step 1 — Identify the distribution of the number of calls per minute.**

The number of calls per minute follows a **Poisson distribution** with parameter $\lambda = 4$. For a Poisson variable:

$$\mu = \lambda = 4, \quad \sigma^2 = \lambda = 4, \quad \sigma = 2$$

**Step 2 — The total number of calls in $n = 50$ intervals is:**

$$T = \sum_{i=1}^{50} X_i$$

By the CLT, $T$ is approximately normal with:

$$E[T] = n\mu = 50 \times 4 = 200$$

$$Var(T) = n\sigma^2 = 50 \times 4 = 200 \implies \sigma_T = \sqrt{200} \approx 14.142$$

**Step 3 — Standardize using the CLT (sum form).**

$$Z = \frac{T - n\mu}{\sigma\sqrt{n}} = \frac{220 - 200}{\sqrt{200}} = \frac{20}{14.142} \approx 1.414$$

**Step 4 — Compute the required probability.**

$$P(T > 220) = P(Z > 1.414) = 1 - \Phi(1.414)$$

From standard normal tables, $\Phi(1.41) \approx 0.9207$, so $\Phi(1.414) \approx 0.9213$.

$$P(T > 220) \approx 1 - 0.9213 = 0.0787$$

**Final Answer:** $P(T > 220) \approx 0.0787$ (i.e., about **7.87%**).

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — *On Properties of Estimators*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the following properties of estimators and explain their significance with one example each:
1. **Unbiasedness**
2. **Consistency**
3. **Efficiency**

**(b) [7 Marks]** Two unbiased estimators $\hat{\theta}_1$ and $\hat{\theta}_2$ of a parameter $\theta$ have variances $Var(\hat{\theta}_1) = 4$ and $Var(\hat{\theta}_2) = 9$. Determine which estimator is more efficient and by how much. Also, compute the **Mean Square Error (MSE)** of each estimator.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q. B (a)

**1. Unbiasedness:**
An estimator $\hat{\theta}$ of a parameter $\theta$ is called **unbiased** if its expected value equals the true parameter value:
$$E[\hat{\theta}] = \theta$$
**Significance:** An unbiased estimator, on average across many samples, hits the true value. It carries no systematic error.
*Example:* The sample mean $\bar{X}_n$ is an unbiased estimator of the population mean $\mu$, since $E[\bar{X}_n] = \mu$.

**2. Consistency:**
An estimator $\hat{\theta}_n$ is **consistent** for $\theta$ if, as the sample size grows, the estimator converges in probability to $\theta$:
$$\forall \epsilon > 0, \quad \lim_{n \to \infty} P(\vert \hat{\theta}_n - \theta \vert < \epsilon) = 1$$
**Significance:** A consistent estimator becomes *more accurate* as more data is collected. It is a "big data" property.
*Example:* The sample mean $\bar{X}_n$ is a consistent estimator of $\mu$ because $Var(\bar{X}_n) = \sigma^2/n \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$, combined with unbiasedness (by Chebyshev's inequality).

**3. Efficiency:**
Among all unbiased estimators of $\theta$, the one with the **smallest variance** is the **most efficient** estimator. If $\hat{\theta}_1$ and $\hat{\theta}_2$ are both unbiased, the **relative efficiency** is:
$$\text{eff}(\hat{\theta}_1, \hat{\theta}_2) = \frac{Var(\hat{\theta}_2)}{Var(\hat{\theta}_1)}$$
**Significance:** Efficiency quantifies *precision*: a more efficient estimator produces estimates that are tightly clustered around the true value.
*Example:* For a normal population with known variance, the sample mean $\bar{X}_n$ is the MVUE for $\mu$ — no other unbiased estimator has smaller variance.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q. B (b)

**Step 1 — Identify the given information.**

Both estimators are unbiased: $E[\hat{\theta}_1] = E[\hat{\theta}_2] = \theta$.

$$Var(\hat{\theta}_1) = 4, \quad Var(\hat{\theta}_2) = 9$$

**Step 2 — Compute the relative efficiency of $\hat{\theta}_1$ with respect to $\hat{\theta}_2$.**

$$\text{eff}(\hat{\theta}_1, \hat{\theta}_2) = \frac{Var(\hat{\theta}_2)}{Var(\hat{\theta}_1)} = \frac{9}{4} = 2.25$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the result.**

Since $\text{eff}(\hat{\theta}_1, \hat{\theta}_2) = 2.25 > 1$, estimator $\hat{\theta}_1$ is **more efficient** than $\hat{\theta}_2$. In fact, $\hat{\theta}_1$ is **2.25 times more efficient** than $\hat{\theta}_2$, meaning it has a substantially tighter sampling distribution around $\theta$.

**Step 4 — Compute the Mean Square Error (MSE) for each estimator.**

Using the decomposition $MSE(\hat{\theta}) = Var(\hat{\theta}) + Bias^2(\hat{\theta})$, and since both estimators are **unbiased** (Bias $= 0$):

$$MSE(\hat{\theta}_1) = Var(\hat{\theta}_1) + 0^2 = 4$$

$$MSE(\hat{\theta}_2) = Var(\hat{\theta}_2) + 0^2 = 9$$

**Final Answer:**
* $\hat{\theta}_1$ is more efficient with a relative efficiency of **2.25**.
* $MSE(\hat{\theta}_1) = 4$ and $MSE(\hat{\theta}_2) = 9$.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE MARKS in CLT and Estimator questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "large $n$" condition.** The CLT is an *asymptotic* result. Markers will deduct marks if you blindly apply it for $n < 30$ without justification.
> 2. **Confusing $\sigma$ with $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$.** The standard deviation of $\bar{X}$ is $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$ (the *standard error*), not $\sigma$. Mixing these up leads to a wrong standardization and a chain of incorrect calculations.
> 3. **Forgetting to use the SUM form vs MEAN form correctly.** In CLT-based problems involving totals (e.g., total production in $n$ shifts), use $\sigma\sqrt{n}$, NOT $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$.
> 4. **Using $\bar{X}$ without proving it is unbiased.** Examiners expect you to write $E[\bar{X}] = \mu$ explicitly using linearity, not just state the result.
> 5. **Omitting the "i.i.d." or "finite variance" assumption** while stating the CLT. The theorem is INVALID without these.
> 6. **Writing "$\hat{\theta}$ is consistent" without defining what convergence in probability means.** Be precise.
> 7. **Forgetting units** in numerical CLT problems (e.g., $Z$ is dimensionless, $\bar{X}$ is in cm). A small deduction, but a real one.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must memorize for KTU ESE:**

### Central Limit Theorem
* ⭐ The CLT says **sample means of i.i.d. random variables approach a normal distribution** as $n \to \infty$, regardless of the parent distribution's shape.
* ⭐ Three forms to remember: **Sum, Mean, Proportion**.
* ⭐ Standard error of the mean is $SE = \sigma / \sqrt{n}$.
* ⭐ For Bernoulli/proportion data, the standard error is $\sqrt{p(1-p)/n}$.
* ⭐ Rule of thumb: $n \geq 30$ for moderate skewness; $n \geq 50$ for heavy skewness.
* ⭐ The CLT is the **theoretical justification** for Z-tests, t-tests (with large $n$), confidence intervals, and control charts.

### Estimator Properties
* ⭐ **Unbiasedness:** $E[\hat{\theta}] = \theta$ → *finite-sample* property.
* ⭐ **Consistency:** $\hat{\theta}_n \xrightarrow{P} \theta$ → *large-sample* property, requires $Var(\hat{\theta}_n) \to 0$ plus unbiasedness (or vanishing bias).
* ⭐ **Efficiency:** Smallest variance among unbiased estimators → *MVUE*.
* ⭐ **Sufficiency:** Statistic $T$ contains ALL information about $\theta$ → use the **Fisher–Neyman Factorization Theorem**.
* ⭐ **MSE = Variance + Bias²** → single scalar loss for comparing any two estimators.
* ⭐ **Cramér–Rao Lower Bound (CRLB):** $Var(\hat{\theta}) \geq 1 / [n \cdot \mathcal{I}(\theta)]$ — fundamental lower bound on estimator variance.

### Numerical Problem Checklist
* ⭐ Always state the form of CLT being used.
* ⭐ Compute $\mu$, $\sigma$, and $SE$ separately and **show intermediate steps**.
* ⭐ Standardize to $Z$ and report the answer using $\Phi(Z)$.
* ⭐ For MSE/CRLB questions, write the **decomposition formula** before substituting values.

---

> ✅ **End of Module 2 Notes — Central Limit Theorem, Estimators: Properties**
> *Prepared in alignment with KTU 2024 Scheme, Revised Bloom's Taxonomy, and Board Examiner conventions.*
