<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`
**Define the term "significance of correlation". Why is it necessary to test the significance of a correlation coefficient?**

**Model Answer:**

The **significance of correlation** refers to the statistical procedure of determining whether the observed sample correlation coefficient ($r$) is large enough to infer that a genuine linear relationship exists in the population, or whether it is attributable to random sampling fluctuation.

**Necessity of Testing:**
1. A non-zero $r$ in a sample does not automatically imply $\rho \neq 0$ in the population.
2. Even uncorrelated populations can produce non-zero sample $r$ due to chance.
3. Significance testing quantifies the probability ($p$-value) of observing such an $r$ under the null hypothesis $\rho = 0$.
4. It enables data-driven decisions by providing a probabilistic basis for rejecting or accepting observed patterns.

> **Examiner's Tip:** Define the term first, then list **at least three** reasons. Full 3 marks require clear logical flow.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`
**State the null and alternative hypotheses for testing the significance of a correlation coefficient. Write the formula for the test statistic.**

**Model Answer:**

**Hypotheses (Two-tailed case):**
$$\begin{aligned}
H_0 &: \rho = 0 \quad \text{(no linear relationship in the population)} \\
H_1 &: \rho \neq 0 \quad \text{(a linear relationship exists in the population)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Test Statistic:**
$$t = \frac{r\sqrt{n-2}}{\sqrt{1-r^{2}}}$$

This statistic follows a Student's $t$-distribution with **$df = n - 2$** under $H_0$.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Degrees of Freedom Mistake:** Most students write $df = n$ instead of $n - 2$. This single error costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **One-tailed vs Two-tailed Confusion:** Always check whether the problem specifies a direction. Default is two-tailed unless stated otherwise.
> 3. **Missing Conclusion:** Computing $t_{calc}$ without stating whether $H_0$ is rejected loses **3 marks**. Always end with a clear English sentence.
> 4. **Skipping Assumption Check:** Failing to mention the bivariate normality assumption costs **1 mark** in full-mark answers.
> 5. **Decimal Precision:** KTU allows 3–4 decimal places; avoid rounding too early in the calculation chain.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

The marks obtained by 8 students in Mathematics ($X$) and Statistics ($Y$) are given below:

| Student | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $X$ | 56 | 62 | 48 | 70 | 64 | 50 | 72 | 60 |
| $Y$ | 60 | 65 | 50 | 72 | 68 | 55 | 78 | 64 |

**(a)** Compute the Pearson correlation coefficient $r$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the correlation is significant. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a): Computation of $r$

**Step 1 — Compute means:**

$$\bar{X} = \frac{56+62+48+70+64+50+72+60}{8} = \frac{482}{8} = 60.25$$

$$\bar{Y} = \frac{60+65+50+72+68+55+78+64}{8} = \frac{512}{8} = 64.00$$

**Step 2 — Compute deviations and products:**

| $i$ | $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $y_i - \bar{y}$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ | $(y_i - \bar{y})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 56 | 60 | $-4.25$ | $-4.00$ | $17.00$ | $18.0625$ | $16.00$ |
| 2 | 62 | 65 | $1.75$ | $1.00$ | $1.75$ | $3.0625$ | $1.00$ |
| 3 | 48 | 50 | $-12.25$ | $-14.00$ | $171.50$ | $150.0625$ | $196.00$ |
| 4 | 70 | 72 | $9.75$ | $8.00$ | $78.00$ | $95.0625$ | $64.00$ |
| 5 | 64 | 68 | $3.75$ | $4.00$ | $15.00$ | $14.0625$ | $16.00$ |
| 6 | 50 | 55 | $-10.25$ | $-9.00$ | $92.25$ | $105.0625$ | $81.00$ |
| 7 | 72 | 78 | $11.75$ | $14.00$ | $164.50$ | $138.0625$ | $196.00$ |
| 8 | 60 | 64 | $-0.25$ | $0.00$ | $0.00$ | $0.0625$ | $0.00$ |
| **Sum** |  |  |  |  | **$540.00$** | **$523.50$** | **$570.00$** |

**Step 3 — Apply formula:**

$$r = \frac{540.00}{\sqrt{523.50 \times 570.00}} = \frac{540.00}{\sqrt{298395.00}} = \frac{540.00}{546.257}$$

$$r \approx 0.9885$$

**Interpretation:** A very strong positive correlation exists between Mathematics and Statistics marks in this sample.

#### Part (b): Significance Test

**Step 1 — Hypotheses:**
$$\begin{aligned}
H_0 &: \rho = 0 \\
H_1 &: \rho \neq 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $t_{calc}$:**

$$t = \frac{0.9885 \sqrt{8-2}}{\sqrt{1-0.9885^2}} = \frac{0.9885 \times \sqrt{6}}{\sqrt{1 - 0.97713}}$$

Numerator: $0.9885 \times 2.4495 = 2.4213$.

Denominator: $\sqrt{0.02287} = 0.1512$.

$$t_{calc} = \frac{2.4213}{0.1512} \approx 16.02$$

**Step 3 — Find $t_{crit}$:**

At $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-tailed), $df = 6$:
$$t_{crit} = \pm 2.447$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**
$$|t_{calc}| = 16.02 \gg t_{crit} = 2.447$$

Therefore, **reject $H_0$**.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
There is overwhelming statistical evidence at the 5% significance level to conclude that a **highly significant linear relationship** exists between Mathematics and Statistics marks in the population.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the procedure for testing the significance of a correlation coefficient with a worked example. State all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a study of 15 paired observations, the computed Pearson correlation coefficient is $r = 0.42$. Test at $\alpha = 0.01$ (two-tailed) whether the correlation is significant. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a): Procedure and Assumptions

**Assumptions Required:**
1. Both variables $X$ and $Y$ are **quantitative** and measured on an interval/ratio scale.
2. The pair $(X, Y)$ follows a **bivariate normal distribution**.
3. The relationship between $X$ and $Y$ is **linear**.
4. Observations are **independently sampled**.

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. Compute the sample correlation coefficient $r$ from the data.
2. State hypotheses: $H_0: \rho = 0$ vs $H_1: \rho \neq 0$.
3. Choose significance level $\alpha$.
4. Compute test statistic $t = \dfrac{r\sqrt{n-2}}{\sqrt{1-r^2}}$ with $df = n - 2$.
5. Compare $|t_{calc}|$ with $t_{crit}$ from the $t$-table.
6. **Decision Rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $|t_{calc}| > t_{crit}$.
7. Conclude in plain English.

**Worked Mini-Example:** Suppose $n = 25$ and $r = 0.6$.

$$t = \frac{0.6 \sqrt{23}}{\sqrt{1-0.36}} = \frac{0.6 \times 4.7958}{\sqrt{0.64}} = \frac{2.8775}{0.8} = 3.597$$

With $df = 23$, $t_{crit}$ at $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-tailed) $\approx 2.069$. Since $3.597 > 2.069$, reject $H_0$ — the correlation is significant.

#### Part (b): Significance Test for $r = 0.42$, $n = 15$, $\alpha = 0.01$

**Step 1 — Hypotheses:**
$$\begin{aligned}
H_0 &: \rho = 0 \\
H_1 &: \rho \neq 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $t_{calc}$:**

$$t = \frac{0.42 \sqrt{15-2}}{\sqrt{1-0.42^{2}}} = \frac{0.42 \times \sqrt{13}}{\sqrt{1-0.1764}}$$

Numerator: $0.42 \times 3.6056 = 1.5144$.

Denominator: $\sqrt{0.8236} = 0.9075$.

$$t_{calc} = \frac{1.5144}{0.9075} \approx 1.669$$

**Step 3 — Find $t_{crit}$:**

At $\alpha = 0.01$ (two-tailed), $df = 13$:
$$t_{crit} = \pm 3.012$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**
$$|t_{calc}| = 1.669 < t_{crit} = 3.012$$

Therefore, **fail to reject $H_0$**.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
At the 1% significance level, the observed correlation of $r = 0.42$ from $n = 15$ observations is **NOT statistically significant**. The data do not provide sufficient evidence to conclude that a linear relationship exists in the population, although the moderate sample value suggests a possible relationship that might emerge as significant with a larger sample.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Significance of Correlation**

- **Definition:** Significance testing evaluates whether an observed sample $r$ reflects a true population correlation $\rho \neq 0$, or mere random fluctuation.
- **Key Symbol Distinction:** Sample statistic is **$r$**; population parameter is **$\rho$** (rho).
- **Test Statistic Formula:** 
$$t = \frac{r\sqrt{n-2}}{\sqrt{1-r^{2}}}$$
- **Degrees of Freedom:** Always **$df = n - 2$** — never $n$ or $n - 1$.
- **Distribution:** Student's $t$-distribution under $H_0$.
- **Default Test:** Two-tailed ($H_1: \rho \neq 0$) unless problem specifies direction.
- **Common $\alpha$ Levels:** $0.05$ (most KTU problems), $0.01$ (stricter), $0.10$ (exploratory).
- **Decision Rule (Critical Value):** Reject $H_0$ if $|t_{calc}| > t_{crit}$.
- **Decision Rule (p-value):** Reject $H_0$ if $p < \alpha$.
- **Assumptions:** Bivariate normality, linearity, random sampling.
- **Significance $\neq$ Strength:** Always report the $r$ value alongside the significance verdict.
- **Sample Size Effect:** Larger $n$ makes weaker correlations become statistically significant; small $n$ may mask real correlations.
- **Fisher $z$-Transform:** Used for confidence intervals when $r$ is large ($z = 0.5 \ln((1+r)/(1-r))$).
- **Always Visualize First:** Plot a scatter plot before computing $r$ — the test assumes linearity.
- **Conclusion Format:** State the decision in plain English — *"There is sufficient evidence at the 5% level to conclude..."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation Using the Regression Line
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET501 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Estimation Using the Regression Line

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Simple Linear Regression, once the least-squares regression line $\hat{Y} = b_0 + b_1 X$ is fitted to a sample of $n$ paired observations $(X_i, Y_i)$, the fitted line serves as an **estimator** for the unknown true population regression line $\mu_{Y \vert X} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X$. The act of using the fitted line to produce numerical values (either averages or future observations) corresponding to a specific value of the predictor $X = x_0$ is formally called **Estimation Using the Regression Line**.

There are two fundamentally distinct estimation tasks:

1. **Estimation of the Mean Response** (Confidence Interval for $E[Y \vert X = x_0]$):
   Estimating the *average* value of $Y$ that would be observed if the experiment were repeated many times at $X = x_0$.

2. **Estimation of an Individual Response** (Prediction Interval for a single new $Y$ at $X = x_0$):
   Estimating a *single new* observation $Y$ at $X = x_0$, which carries additional randomness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The single most-tested distinction in board exams is the difference between a **Confidence Interval (CI)** for the mean response and a **Prediction Interval (PI)** for a new individual response. A PI is always wider than a CI for the same $x_0$ because it incorporates both the uncertainty in estimating the line *and* the inherent variability of individual $Y$ values around the line.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **fisherman** studying the relationship between the *length of a fishing net* ($X$) and the *weight of fish caught* ($Y$). After plotting data from 30 days, you fit a straight line: $\widehat{\text{Weight}} = 2 + 0.5 \times (\text{Net Length})$.

- **Confidence Interval (CI):** "On average, across many days, if I use a **100-metre net**, the *average* fish weight I can expect to catch is roughly 52 kg, give or take ±2 kg." This estimates the *mean* of all possible catches for that net length.
- **Prediction Interval (PI):** "If I use a **100-metre net tomorrow**, my single catch will be roughly 52 kg, give or take ±6 kg." This estimates *one specific catch* — and the wider interval reflects the natural day-to-day luck (waves, fish migration, weather).

The line is the same, but the *use case* changes the width of the interval.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Vocabulary Anchors:**
> - $S_e$ : Standard Error of the Estimate (residual standard error).
> - $\bar{X}$, $S_{XX}$ : Sample mean and corrected sum of squares of $X$.
> - $x_0$ : The specific predictor value at which estimation is performed.
> - $t_{\alpha/2,\,n-2}$ : Critical value from the Student's $t$-distribution with $n-2$ degrees of freedom.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Foundational Quantities

Before any interval can be constructed, the analyst must compute three building blocks:

**Step 1 — Standard Error of the Estimate ($S_e$):**
This quantifies the *typical vertical distance* between observed $Y$-values and the fitted regression line. It is the square root of the **Mean Square Error (MSE)**.

$$S_e = \sqrt{\text{MSE}} = \sqrt{\frac{\text{SSE}}{n-2}} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2}{n-2}}$$

**Step 2 — Variance of $\hat{Y}$ at a point $x_0$ (for the Mean Response):**
This measures the uncertainty in estimating the *height of the line* at $X = x_0$. It is smallest at $\bar{X}$ and grows quadratically as $x_0$ moves away from the mean.

$$\text{Var}(\hat{Y}_0) = S_e^2 \left[ \frac{1}{n} + \frac{(x_0 - \bar{X})^2}{S_{XX}} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Variance for a new individual observation (for the Prediction):**
This adds the irreducible random error of a single $Y$ to the variance of the line itself.

$$\text{Var}(Y_0 - \hat{Y}_0) = S_e^2 \left[ 1 + \frac{1}{n} + \frac{(x_0 - \bar{X})^2}{S_{XX}} \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the "+1" matters:** The prediction interval contains an extra $S_e^2$ because predicting *one* outcome includes the natural scatter of $Y$ around its mean — there is no averaging benefit. This single algebraic difference is the *most heavily tested concept* in KTU 2024 module-end exams.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Use Case |
|---|----------|---------|----------|
| 1 | Standard Error of Estimate | $S_e = \sqrt{\dfrac{\text{SSE}}{n-2}}$ | Base uncertainty of regression |
| 2 | Sum of Squared Errors | $\text{SSE} = \text{SST} - \text{SSR} = S_{YY} - b_1 S_{XY}$ | Component of $S_e$ |
| 3 | Variance of Mean Estimate | $S_e^2 \left[\dfrac{1}{n} + \dfrac{(x_0-\bar{X})^2}{S_{XX}}\right]$ | Building block for **CI** |
| 4 | Variance of New Prediction | $S_e^2 \left[1 + \dfrac{1}{n} + \dfrac{(x_0-\bar{X})^2}{S_{XX}}\right]$ | Building block for **PI** |
| 5 | **CI for Mean Response** at $x_0$ | $\hat{Y}_0 \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,n-2}\cdot S_e \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n} + \dfrac{(x_0-\bar{X})^2}{S_{XX}}}$ | Average $Y$ at $X=x_0$ |
| 6 | **PI for New Observation** at $x_0$ | $\hat{Y}_0 \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,n-2}\cdot S_e \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{1}{n} + \dfrac{(x_0-\bar{X})^2}{S_{XX}}}$ | Single $Y$ at $X=x_0$ |
| 7 | CI for Slope $\beta_1$ | $b_1 \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,n-2}\cdot \dfrac{S_e}{\sqrt{S_{XX}}}$ | Significance of linear trend |
| 8 | CI for Intercept $\beta_0$ | $b_0 \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,n-2}\cdot S_e \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n} + \dfrac{\bar{X}^2}{S_{XX}}}$ | Value of $Y$ when $X=0$ |
| 9 | $t$-test for slope | $t = \dfrac{b_1 - 0}{S_e / \sqrt{S_{XX}}}$ | $H_0 : \beta_1 = 0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In all formulas above, $S_{XX} = \sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2$ and $S_{YY} = \sum (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2$. Critical: the notation $\vert x_0 - \bar{X} \vert$ should be parsed as the *absolute difference*, not a markdown table separator.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Estimating the *average* compressive strength of concrete batches for a given curing time (CI), and predicting the strength of a *single future* test pour (PI).
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Predicting the fatigue life of a *single* component under a given stress level (PI) versus the *mean* fatigue life of an entire production batch (CI).
- **Industrial Engineering:** Forecasting the average demand for a product given an advertising budget, and the inventory level needed for a single uncertain week.
- **Electronics:** Estimating the mean signal-to-noise ratio at a given temperature, versus the SNR of one specific device on the production line.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Standard Error of the Estimate ($S_e$)

We begin with the *Total Sum of Squares* and partition it:

$$\text{SST} = \text{SSR} + \text{SSE}$$

**Step 1 — Express SSR using the slope:**

$$\text{SSR} = b_1 \, S_{XY} = b_1 \sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})$$

**Step 2 — Since $b_1 = S_{XY}/S_{XX}$, substitute back:**

$$\text{SSR} = \frac{S_{XY}^2}{S_{XX}}$$

**Step 3 — Express SSE as the unexplained part:**

$$\text{SSE} = \text{SST} - \text{SSR} = S_{YY} - \frac{S_{XY}^2}{S_{XX}}$$

**Step 4 — The unbiased estimator for the error variance $\sigma^2$ uses $n-2$ degrees of freedom** (because two parameters $b_0$ and $b_1$ were estimated):

$$S_e^2 = \hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{\text{SSE}}{n-2} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad S_e = \sqrt{\frac{\text{SSE}}{n-2}}$$

This $S_e$ is the cornerstone of *every* interval-estimation formula in the regression line.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Confidence Interval for the Mean Response $E[Y \vert X = x_0]$

**Step 1 — The point estimator** of the mean response is the fitted value:

$$\hat{Y}_0 = b_0 + b_1 x_0$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $b_0 = \bar{Y} - b_1 \bar{X}$:**

$$\hat{Y}_0 = \bar{Y} - b_1 \bar{X} + b_1 x_0 = \bar{Y} + b_1 (x_0 - \bar{X})$$

**Step 3 — Compute the variance of $\hat{Y}_0$:**

Since $\bar{Y}$ and $b_1$ are independent and $b_1$ has variance $\sigma^2 / S_{XX}$:

$$\text{Var}(\hat{Y}_0) = \text{Var}(\bar{Y}) + (x_0 - \bar{X})^2 \,\text{Var}(b_1)$$

$$\text{Var}(\hat{Y}_0) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n} + (x_0 - \bar{X})^2 \cdot \frac{\sigma^2}{S_{XX}}$$

**Step 4 — Factor out $\sigma^2$ and replace it with the estimator $S_e^2$:**

$$\widehat{\text{Var}}(\hat{Y}_0) = S_e^2 \left[ \frac{1}{n} + \frac{(x_0 - \bar{X})^2}{S_{XX}} \right]$$

**Step 5 — The pivot statistic** follows a $t$-distribution with $n-2$ degrees of freedom:

$$T = \frac{\hat{Y}_0 - E[Y \vert X = x_0]}{S_e \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n} + \dfrac{(x_0 - \bar{X})^2}{S_{XX}}}} \sim t_{n-2}$$

**Step 6 — Invert the probability statement** $P(-t_{\alpha/2} \le T \le t_{\alpha/2}) = 1 - \alpha$ to obtain the **CI for the mean response**:

$$\boxed{\;\hat{Y}_0 \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,n-2} \cdot S_e \sqrt{\frac{1}{n} + \frac{(x_0 - \bar{X})^2}{S_{XX}}}\;}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Prediction Interval for a New Individual Observation

**Step 1 — A new observation is modelled as:**

$$Y_0 = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_0 + \varepsilon_0, \quad \varepsilon_0 \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$$

**Step 2 — The prediction error** is the difference between the new $Y_0$ and the fitted $\hat{Y}_0$:

$$\text{Error} = Y_0 - \hat{Y}_0 = (\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_0 + \varepsilon_0) - (b_0 + b_1 x_0)$$

**Step 3 — The variance of this prediction error:**

The future error $\varepsilon_0$ is independent of $\hat{Y}_0$ (a different realisation), so variances *add*:

$$\text{Var}(Y_0 - \hat{Y}_0) = \text{Var}(\hat{Y}_0) + \text{Var}(\varepsilon_0) = S_e^2\left[\frac{1}{n} + \frac{(x_0-\bar{X})^2}{S_{XX}}\right] + \sigma^2$$

**Step 4 — Replace $\sigma^2$ with $S_e^2$:**

$$\widehat{\text{Var}}(Y_0 - \hat{Y}_0) = S_e^2 \left[ 1 + \frac{1}{n} + \frac{(x_0 - \bar{X})^2}{S_{XX}} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Construct the $t$-pivot** (the future error is normal, and so is the line):

$$T = \frac{Y_0 - \hat{Y}_0}{S_e \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{1}{n} + \dfrac{(x_0-\bar{X})^2}{S_{XX}}}} \sim t_{n-2}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $Y_0$ to obtain the Prediction Interval:**

$$\boxed{\;\hat{Y}_0 \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,n-2} \cdot S_e \sqrt{1 + \frac{1}{n} + \frac{(x_0 - \bar{X})^2}{S_{XX}}}\;}$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Comprehensive)

**Problem Statement (Modeled on KTU University Exam Pattern):**
A study on the relationship between **temperature** ($X$, in °C) and **yield of a chemical reaction** ($Y$, in grams) gives the following sample statistics for $n = 10$ observations:

$$\bar{X} = 60, \quad \bar{Y} = 40, \quad S_{XX} = 240, \quad S_{XY} = 180, \quad S_{YY} = 200$$

The fitted regression line is $\hat{Y} = -5 + 0.75 X$. Compute a **95% CI for the mean yield at $x_0 = 70$ °C** and a **95% PI for a new individual batch at $x_0 = 70$ °C**. Given $t_{0.025, 8} = 2.306$.

---

**Sub-problem 1 — Compute $S_e$:**

**Step A:** Compute SSR:

$$\text{SSR} = \frac{S_{XY}^2}{S_{XX}} = \frac{180^2}{240} = \frac{32400}{240} = 135$$

**Step B:** Compute SSE:

$$\text{SSE} = S_{YY} - \text{SSR} = 200 - 135 = 65$$

**Step C:** Compute $S_e$:

$$S_e = \sqrt{\frac{\text{SSE}}{n-2}} = \sqrt{\frac{65}{8}} = \sqrt{8.125} = 2.8507$$

---

**Sub-problem 2 — Compute the fitted value at $x_0 = 70$:**

$$\hat{Y}_0 = -5 + 0.75 \times 70 = -5 + 52.5 = 47.5 \text{ grams}$$

---

**Sub-problem 3 — 95% CI for the mean response at $x_0 = 70$:**

**Step A:** Compute the standard error of $\hat{Y}_0$:

$$SE(\hat{Y}_0) = S_e \sqrt{\frac{1}{n} + \frac{(x_0 - \bar{X})^2}{S_{XX}}}$$

$$SE(\hat{Y}_0) = 2.8507 \sqrt{\frac{1}{10} + \frac{(70-60)^2}{240}}$$

$$SE(\hat{Y}_0) = 2.8507 \sqrt{0.1 + \frac{100}{240}}$$

$$SE(\hat{Y}_0) = 2.8507 \sqrt{0.1 + 0.4167}$$

$$SE(\hat{Y}_0) = 2.8507 \sqrt{0.5167} = 2.8507 \times 0.7188 = 2.0492$$

**Step B:** Apply the CI formula:

$$\text{CI} = 47.5 \pm 2.306 \times 2.0492 = 47.5 \pm 4.7254$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{95\% CI} = (42.77,\; 52.23) \text{ grams}\;}$$

**Interpretation:** We are 95% confident that the *true average* yield of the chemical reaction at 70°C lies between **42.77 g and 52.23 g**.

---

**Sub-problem 4 — 95% PI for a new individual batch at $x_0 = 70$:**

**Step A:** Compute the standard error for prediction:

$$SE_{\text{pred}} = S_e \sqrt{1 + \frac{1}{n} + \frac{(x_0 - \bar{X})^2}{S_{XX}}}$$

$$SE_{\text{pred}} = 2.8507 \sqrt{1 + 0.1 + 0.4167} = 2.8507 \sqrt{1.5167}$$

$$SE_{\text{pred}} = 2.8507 \times 1.2316 = 3.5108$$

**Step B:** Apply the PI formula:

$$\text{PI} = 47.5 \pm 2.306 \times 3.5108 = 47.5 \pm 8.0959$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{95\% PI} = (39.40,\; 55.60) \text{ grams}\;}$$

**Interpretation:** A single new batch at 70°C has a 95% chance of yielding between **39.40 g and 55.60 g**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Verification of the Rule:** The PI width is $16.19$, the CI width is $9.45$. The PI is **~1.71× wider** than the CI, which is the expected signature of the additional "$+1$" inside the square root. Students are advised to verify this ratio in every exam problem.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Interval

| Scenario | Symbol | Formula Component | Typical KTU Question Trigger Phrase |
|----------|--------|-------------------|-------------------------------------|
| "Average value of $Y$..." | CI | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{n} + \frac{(x_0-\bar{X})^2}{S_{XX}}}$ | "mean response", "expected value", "average yield" |
| "A single new value of $Y$..." | PI | $\sqrt{1 + \frac{1}{n} + \frac{(x_0-\bar{X})^2}{S_{XX}}}$ | "next observation", "predict a single", "one more item" |
| "Significance of slope" | $t$-test | $\frac{b_1}{S_e / \sqrt{S_{XX}}}$ | "test if $X$ affects $Y$", "linear relationship exists" |
| "Confidence band for the entire line" | Working-Hotelling | $\pm W \cdot S_e \sqrt{\frac{1}{n} + \frac{(x-\bar{X})^2}{S_{XX}}}$ | "confidence band", "simultaneous interval for all $x$" |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow of Estimation Using a Regression Line

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sample Data X sub i and Y sub i] --> B[Compute S sub XX, S sub XY, S sub YY]
    B --> C[Fit Least Squares Line Y hat equals b0 plus b1 X]
    C --> D[Compute SSE equals SYY minus SSR]
    D --> E[Compute Standard Error S sub e]
    E --> F{Choose Estimation Goal}
    F --> G1[Mean Response at x0]
    F --> G2[New Individual Y at x0]
    F --> G3[Test Significance of Slope]
    G1 --> H1[Use SE of Y hat with 1 by n term only]
    G2 --> H2[Use SE of Y hat with 1 plus 1 by n term]
    G3 --> H3[Compute t equals b1 over SE of b1]
    H1 --> I1[Construct CI for Mean]
    H2 --> I2[Construct PI for Individual]
    H3 --> I3[Compare t to critical t for conclusion]
    I1 --> J[Final Decision and Interpretation]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
```

### 4.2 Visual Geometry of CI vs PI (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph linefit[Fitted Regression Line Geometry]
        direction TB
        nodeP[Point X equals x0 on X axis] --> nodeYhat[Compute Y hat zero on the line]
        nodeYhat --> nodeCI[CI bracket lies around the line]
        nodeYhat --> nodePI[PI bracket lies around a future point]
    end
    nodeCI --> labelCI[CI is narrow because only line uncertainty]
    nodePI --> labelPI[PI is wider because of additional scatter]
    labelCI --> compare[(PI half-width) greater than (CI half-width)]
    labelPI --> compare
    compare --> note[Extra term plus 1 inside square root is the cause]
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree: When to Use Which Interval

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[You have a fitted regression line and a target x0] --> q1{Is the goal to estimate the MEAN Y or a SINGLE Y?}
    q1 -->|Mean of many Y values| ci[Construct Confidence Interval]
    q1 -->|One specific new Y value| pi[Construct Prediction Interval]
    ci --> formulaCI[Use formula with 1 by n plus squared deviation term]
    pi --> formulaPI[Use formula with 1 plus 1 by n plus squared deviation term]
    formulaCI --> check[Verify S sub e, degrees of freedom, t critical value]
    formulaPI --> check
    check --> done[Report the interval with proper units and interpretation]
```
